### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-07T15:05:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/close-to-suicide-adrenal-problems-ruining-my-life.5869/post-133386>

When AF is in the final phases it cause a loss of the NMDA receptors in the long distance tracts of the brain designed to have the most DHA......as the DHA is depleted by the ROS and RNS it fast forwards and the ca2+ efflux (better known as excitotoxicity) cause glutamate excess that destroys NMDA. This is when disturbances in thought, consciousness and perception folllow. It is the final common pathway for most prion disease and mental illness and those with poor thoughts tied to low dopamine levels in the brain

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-07-09T09:01:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/close-to-suicide-adrenal-problems-ruining-my-life.5869/post-133667>

isotretinoin induces [apoptosis](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apoptosis) (cell death) in various cells in the body. Cell death may be instigated in the [meibomian glands](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meibomian_gland),[\[58\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isotretinoin#cite_note-Lambert_1989-58)[\[59\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isotretinoin#cite_note-Kremer1994-59) [hypothalamic](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hypothalamic) cells,[\[60\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isotretinoin#cite_note-Griffin_2010-60) [hippocampus](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hippocampus) cells[\[61\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isotretinoin#cite_note-Crandall2004-61)[\[62\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isotretinoin#cite_note-Sakai_2004-62)

i was put on roaccutane as a teen, worst shit ever...hippocampal apoptosis can accoung for the brain fog/memory issues!

# Thread: Heart Attack Grill's 'spokesman' dies of heart attack
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-attack-grills-spokesman-dies-of-heart-attack.5870/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ATL_Paleo — 2013-02-12T23:54:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-attack-grills-spokesman-dies-of-heart-attack.5870/post-75610>

The unofficial spokesman for the infamous Las Vegas restaurant Heart Attack Grill has, you guessed it, died of a heart attack.

John Alleman, 52, was waiting for a bus in front of the restaurant last week when he suffered cardiac arrest and was removed from life support Monday afternoon, the restaurant's owner Jon Basso told the Las Vegas Sun.

The Heart Attack Grill's slogan is "Taste Worth Dying For" and it is known for it's medical theme and high-caloric menu, which features items like the **record-breaking 9,982-calorie, 3-pound Quadruple Bypass Burger.**

The restaurant features waitresses dressed as sexy nurses and has a "diet program" where they "prescribe great-tasting, high-calorie meals including the Double Bypass Burger, Flatliner Fries, Full Sugar Coke, Butterfat Shake and no-filter cigarettes!"

[http://www.nypost.com/p/news/national/heart\_attack\_grill\_spokesman\_heart\_tJ5F4LMP9eWdsh1JnDpw4O](http://www.nypost.com/p/news/national/heart_attack_grill_spokesman_heart_tJ5F4LMP9eWdsh1JnDpw4O)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Does type of protein matter
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-type-of-protein-matter.5871/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Reset 2013
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### andee — 2013-02-13T06:43:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-type-of-protein-matter.5871/post-75669>

I'm noticing that certain types of protein seem to keep me satisfied longer - has anyone else had this experience? I could pack away 60 grams of shrimp for my BAB and be hungry an hour later - even adding coconut oil to my coffee and putting butter on the shrimp. 50 grams beef will keep me sated for 3-4 hours. 50 grams Bacon/eggs/lamb will work for 6 or 7 hours. I'm using the heavier proteins for the BAB, which seems to work, but am just wondering if anyone else notices this.

This is a great forum - thanks everyone.

### KiwiLauren — 2013-02-13T06:52:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-type-of-protein-matter.5871/post-75670>

> [andee said:](/goto/post?id=75669)
>
> I'm noticing that certain types of protein seem to keep me satisfied longer - has anyone else had this experience? I could pack away 60 mg of shrimp for my BAB and be hungry an hour later - even adding coconut oil to my coffee and putting butter on the shrimp. 50 mg beef will keep me sated for 3-4 hours. 50 mg Bacon/eggs/lamb will work for 6 or 7 hours. I'm using the heavier proteins for the BAB, which seems to work, but am just wondering if anyone else notices this.
>
> This is a great forum - thanks everyone.

Andee, a lot of people commented on this exact thing when the epi-paleo rx was first released. I tend to do what you do - I eat my saturated fat and meat protein for BAB and (I tend to only eat 2 meals a day) eat my seafood (usually trying to keep it with non-saturated fats) for the second meal. I can't explain why many have this reaction (maybe something to do with the way we burn/use DHA?) but you are not alone.

### andee — 2013-02-13T07:46:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-type-of-protein-matter.5871/post-75672>

KiwiLauren -Thank you! This is a relief. I prefer to eat 2 meals a day as well, and this seems to be the only way to do it.

### caroline — 2013-02-13T08:46:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-type-of-protein-matter.5871/post-75675>

Andee I am pretty sure that Dr. Kruse has said the same thing...... If he is having seafood he will also have some meat.
I do that quite often so that I get seafood in at both meals. I, as well, only have two meals. I hope you are doing really well. Keep us posted on your progress ......it helps everyone.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-13T13:01:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-type-of-protein-matter.5871/post-75691>

yes types do matter a bit........

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-02-13T15:28:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-type-of-protein-matter.5871/post-75694>

I quite like meat and seafood together.  Even if it's bacon and seafood. Sometimes I accidentally only had seafood for too long of a time. Oops.

Beef heart. Since I discovered beef heart in January I almost never want liver any more. It tastes better and doesn't stink up the whole house (got to be considerate to other people living here who gag from liver).

### andee — 2013-02-13T17:03:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-type-of-protein-matter.5871/post-75702>

Thanks, everyone. Bacon and scallops for breakfast, too awesome.

### diane — 2013-02-13T19:44:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-type-of-protein-matter.5871/post-75723>

Second (or 3rd) on having the saturated fat for BAB. Although once I've been doing that for a couple of weeks, I find that I can have seafood - shrimp, salmon, etc - for BAB, but I make sure that I have enough fat with it.

Zorica - just an FYI. Heart is not considered organ meat - it's muscle meat. So if you're trying replace liver, it won't be the same. Heart is great - it's just not organ. At least that's what I have learned from years of feeding raw to my dogs.

### Patty Cakes — 2013-02-13T20:30:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-type-of-protein-matter.5871/post-75732>

...4th or 5th on the sat fat for BAB. It's the only thing that keeps me full enough and then I only want one more meal, seafood, to finish out the day. I have plenty of energy now, don't get hungry at night, and really feel, for right now, this is working for me.

### andee — 2013-02-14T03:39:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-type-of-protein-matter.5871/post-75773>

Very true about saturated fat - good reminder. I had a great day, only 2 meals and more energy. Thanks to all for the support.

# Thread: I need help with smart meter fight!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-smart-meter-fight.5872/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Inger — 2013-02-13T09:59:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-smart-meter-fight.5872/post-75677>

Ok folks, please help. I am fighting about the new installed smart meter, and I need some education..
I got a paper where says at what frequences the signals are, and it is in between 75 kHz - 132 kHz. In my book about radiowaves and how they destroy health, the radiowaves in between 87 MHz - 108 MHz, instead of 70 MHz as they used to be in "old times". The lower frequensies were much less bad for our health. Now, the kHz is way less than MHz, so I figured maybe our smart meter is not too bad? But I do not know enough about this to do a wise desicion! They said there are different smart meters and some have much stronger frequensies, they have a very "safe" one. Hmmm..

I am not sure if it is this but since a while (the meter is here about 2 months) our home telephone makes so weird nose, real loud, for maybe 5 seconds, every 30 second or so. It is so annoying! I wonder if it has to do with the meter. It started around that time the meter was installed I guess.. as it is a new phenomena.

They sent me papers about a filter too, that can be installed. Now I need to find out if I let them meter be and install the filter or what to do. I know way to little about those low frequensies (kHz) to make a desicion... I really do not want to make unnecessary hassle either..

### caroline — 2013-02-13T10:36:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-smart-meter-fight.5872/post-75680>

I wish I could help Inger - but really don't know - someone else is bound to tho.

### BJK77 — 2013-02-13T15:32:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-smart-meter-fight.5872/post-75695>

I wish I could help Inger, but I'm in the same position and am hoping others chime in with some good information. I contacted my electric company 6 months ago to ask about the schedule for installing smart meters and was told they were only done in select houses on a trial basis and it would be years before it reached my area. I was also told we would be notified before the meter was installed.

I just called back in again this morning to follow up and see if they had any newer information and now I'm being told we ALREADY have one!! I am furious. I told them my son has sensitivities to EMF (which I believe to be true) and I want to know how we can have it removed. The person I spoke to was very rude and said there's no such thing as being sensitive to the smart meters because they don't emit any EMF since everything is transmitted through the phone line. She also told me they no longer have any of the analog meters, so we really don't have a choice here, but she'll have someone call me back anyway.

I need to get my facts together and figure out what I'm going to say. It pisses me off to no end that we have no effin rights in this country anymore. They want to tell us what the hell to eat, how to spend our damn $, etc, etc. I am on FIRE right now!!

### shilohman — 2013-02-13T20:45:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-smart-meter-fight.5872/post-75735>

Inger, consider your telephone the equivalent of a canary in a coal mine. Your phone can't die like the canary, but its trying to tell you there is EMF.
What to do:
1) at night have someone take an axe/hammer to the smart meter. Not recommended, but I read that in Germany after the third replacement the power company will put the old meter back up.
2) the meter is attached to the outside of your house. On the inside of your house on the wall the meter is attached to 1) cover the wall with aluminum screen, doesn't have to be big about a square meter or smaller. Now go check your telephone. Hopefully your phone is behaving properly 2) on the outside of your wall install a grounding rod and attach a wire to the rod, run the wire through the wall and attach to the aluminum screen attached to the wall. If you do not have access to aluminum screen you could use a sheet of metal.

The power company may complain if your aluminum shield prevents the smart meter from communicating with the smart meter attached to the house next door, but that is their problem.

### Jude — 2013-02-14T07:22:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-smart-meter-fight.5872/post-75781>

I found this article quite interesting-especially this description on the smart meter.

THE DUTY CYCLE
The data from the wireless Smart Meter is sent out in short transmission bursts, which usually last only
from 12 to 120 milliseconds (ms) at a time. These transmission times are referred to as duty cycles. A
100% duty cycle corresponds to continuous operation, e.g. 24 hours/day. A 1% duty cycle corresponds
to a transmitter operation of 1% per 24 hours, which means 14.4 minutes/day.
The most controversial question in the assessment of wireless Smart Meters is how often is this
intermittent burst occurring per day? This short time sequence makes it difficult to detect and measure
the signal properly with RF instrumentation. The estimates on how often the pulse or burst occurs range
from:
Once or twice a day
Once (1) every four (4) hours
To 750 times/day
To an almost continuous transmission from utility access
points

[http://electricsense.com/wp-content/uploads/private/Smart-Meter-What-We-Know-2-6-12.pdf](http://electricsense.com/wp-content/uploads/private/Smart-Meter-What-We-Know-2-6-12.pdf)

### nonchalant — 2013-02-14T16:43:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-smart-meter-fight.5872/post-75823>

> [shilohman said:](/goto/post?id=75735)
>
> Inger, consider your telephone the equivalent of a canary in a coal mine. Your phone can't die like the canary, but its trying to tell you there is EMF.
> What to do:
> 1) at night have someone take an axe/hammer to the smart meter. Not recommended, but I read that in Germany after the third replacement the power company will put the old meter back up.
> 2) the meter is attached to the outside of your house. On the inside of your house on the wall the meter is attached to 1) cover the wall with aluminum screen, doesn't have to be big about a square meter or smaller. Now go check your telephone. Hopefully your phone is behaving properly 2) on the outside of your wall install a grounding rod and attach a wire to the rod, run the wire through the wall and attach to the aluminum screen attached to the wall. If you do not have access to aluminum screen you could use a sheet of metal.
>
> The power company may complain if your aluminum shield prevents the smart meter from communicating with the smart meter attached to the house next door, but that is their problem.

Shilohman, like the screen from a window or door? That would be easy and cheap! Most screen is plastic now, but I can find some old stuff... Our neighbor's smart meter is visible from our bedroom window. I need to see what that window screen is made of.

### Shijin13 — 2013-02-14T17:23:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-smart-meter-fight.5872/post-75824>

> [Nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=75823)

> Shilohman, like the screen from a window or door? That would be easy and cheap! Most screen is plastic now, but I can find some old stuff... Our neighbor's smart meter is visible from our bedroom window. I need to see what that window screen is made of.

### nonchalant — 2013-02-14T17:32:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-smart-meter-fight.5872/post-75826>

> [EE Dude said:](/goto/post?id=0)

### shilohman — 2013-02-14T17:41:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-smart-meter-fight.5872/post-75828>

> [Nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=75823)
>
> Shilohman, like the screen from a window or door? That would be easy and cheap! Most screen is plastic now, but I can find some old stuff... Our neighbor's smart meter is visible from our bedroom window. I need to see what that window screen is made of.

Yes, you can buy a roll of aluminum screen from Home Depot or any hardware store. You might have to do the whole bedroom wall that faces your neighbor's smart meter. If your window screens are not aluminum, you can replace what you have with aluminum.

# Thread: Oysters, Oysters.....& hey, Oysters
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-oysters-hey-oysters.5877/>
**Subforum:** Epi-Paleo Recipes
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Christy — 2013-02-13T20:47:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-oysters-hey-oysters.5877/post-75737>

ok, so, I'm trying to add oysters to my menus, maybe even up to 8-10 times per week, **IF** I can figure out enough different ways to prepare them. (Can't do raw yet, gut too leaky) And don't want to get bored with them.

But I wanted to be able have some in the fridge already cooked so they can be a quick fix - say with paleo cocktail sauce and lime juice. except they are so delicate that even steaming mad them dry out a tad, so even a little reheat can make them tough.

So I went looking for another way and found out how to poach them - and it worked out so well!

below are links for recipes for both shucked (I buy Hilton Point shucked/raw)

[http://www.coastseafoods.com/shucked.html](http://www.coastseafoods.com/shucked.html)

and they are oh so convenient and still taste fresh! And were soooo good reheated the next day, too!
Here's the poaching recipe I used, (but next going to get creative with the poaching liquid for added flavor!)

[http://www.silverpointoysters.com/recpoached.html](http://www.silverpointoysters.com/recpoached.html)

and here's a poaching recipe if you want to poach them in the shells:

[http://www.newenglandprovisions.com/shellfishrecipes/poachedoysters.html](http://www.newenglandprovisions.com/shellfishrecipes/poachedoysters.html)

**
so I am wondering how everyone's fixing their oysters AND what kinds of sauces you are using on various seafoods, (if any) that might lend itself to oysters, as well???**

thanks!

Christy

# Thread: new   where do I start ?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-where-do-i-start.5879/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### magda — 2013-02-14T00:56:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-where-do-i-start.5879/post-75757>

Hi,

I believe I was suffering from extreme adrenal fatigue (big move, emergency 5x due to mold exposure, almost lost my marriage, child in hospital) and on top of that I homeschool and worked weekends in a high stress environment. I didn't sleep on the weekends and hardly ate anything and then to find out I was pregnant.

Well...on the 13th of January as I was teaching on the weekend I felt like I have been shot in the head and had the sensation that I will fall down backwards. I was disoriented for a minute and was okay afterwards but low concentration, focus, mental clarity...my digestion was shut...I suddenly couldn't eat nor answer basic questions. I slept 3 days straight after that and ended up in emergency for examination due to the fact that one of my pupils was dialated. I have been recovering slowly since then...cut out sugar, white flour, processed foods since then but mentally and physically I feel exhausted...I go to sleep earlier (used to stay awake til 2am) now and feel like I can't get enough rest. I have an appointment with a naturopath tomorrow but finding a doctor here is very difficult due to shortage and I did find one but since I have just miscarriaged she does not want to check my hormones since it's probably out of whack anyway.

I don't know what my problem is but I really believe that I had extreme adrenal fatigue which caused some nerve damage that day on the 13th ?

Where do I go from here ? Is it sufficient to do the leptin protocol and just paleo diet or is my problem now more serious than that. I had hypgoglycemia but since cutting out sugar I don't think I have any signs of it now. I have cut out working and am completely useless now as I just rest from day to day and am still exhausted.

I need direction.

### Shijin13 — 2013-02-14T01:17:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-where-do-i-start.5879/post-75758>

Welcome.

Start small, and focus on the basics.

Leptin Reset, Epi-paleo (seafood is your primary meat source), focus on limiting artificial light, go out side and ground yourself as much as possible, and CT (Start w/face dunks)

Many of here on the forum have been working on our health for years. its a long road - but you will see improvements. Ask questions, and read (print out the blogs!)

### freshveggies — 2013-02-14T01:44:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-where-do-i-start.5879/post-75762>

Check out Dr. Shoemaker-----suggests cholestyramine for mold exposure.

### caroline — 2013-02-14T09:35:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-where-do-i-start.5879/post-75787>

Welcome Magda - it is going to take time.....you didn't get broken overnite. Stick with it very carefully and keep reading - things do become clearer the more you read and pay attention to artificial light - that is really huge.
Keep coming here and asking questions - there are a lot of really smart people here to help....

### diane — 2013-02-14T14:58:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-where-do-i-start.5879/post-75808>

If you think it's adrenal fatigue, here is a blog post that is helpful. [http://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-16-adrenal-fatigue-rx/](http://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-16-adrenal-fatigue-rx/)

Although I don't have it as extreme as you do, the most helpful thing for me has been to start fixing my sleep (increasing melatonin production) by working with meal timing and liminting artificial light after dark.

I like what Shijin said - small steps, start with things that are easy to implement and then add things along the way!

### nonchalant — 2013-02-14T16:36:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-where-do-i-start.5879/post-75822>

> [magda said:](/goto/post?id=75757)
>
> I had hypgoglycemia but since cutting out sugar I don't think I have any signs of it now.

Sounds like you have already taken a huge step in the right direction, Magda. Remember that bread is also a sugar. Well, give the body a few seconds and then it's sugar.

### magda — 2013-02-23T02:54:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-where-do-i-start.5879/post-77193>

I have started feeling better...can sleep at night again...though I go to sleep early around 9-10pm and turn lights down around 5pm and my kids go to sleep around 8pm. I am taking vit C from a whole food source, vit D, adrenal refresh, grounded black seeds and flax seeds, garlic, tumeric in capsules and first and foremost have been praying alot. I still don't eat processed anything but have had a few things once in awhile here and there...today however I feel like I am either experiencing some anxiety and I can feel it all mentally and my pupil was a lil more dilated again earlier this morning or I have some neurological issues...I have no idea...maybe my mental state is still kind of slow and is not there yet...what type of doctor does everyone here go to ? A neurologist or a naturopath ? What kind of testings are recommended ?
Also, I weigh about 150 though I look more like 130 but I have abdominal fat after the birth of my last child when alot of stress has started for me due to life situations and being overwhelmed with the people around me. Is the abdominal fat a sign ? And how do I get rid of it if I can't work out due to adrenal fatigue or have cut out processed foods over a month ago ? I am also 30.

### caroline — 2013-02-23T03:01:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-where-do-i-start.5879/post-77197>

Have you had an ASI done? any testing at all? testing is important to get a baseline and then go from there. Your hormones are very likely out of wack. Have you had a consult with Dr. kruse? It sounds like you have a lot of demands on you .... keep searching for answers you will get there!

### ATL_Paleo — 2013-02-23T03:41:21+0200

[http://www.jackkruse.com/getting-started-guide/](http://www.jackkruse.com/getting-started-guide/)

### diane — 2013-02-23T04:15:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-where-do-i-start.5879/post-77211>

I've read so many things that are a cause of abdominal fat - excess cortisol, excess estrogen. Getting some of the basic hormone tests and as caroline mentioned an ASI can help you understand.

When I start I find that it can take a few weeks to get over the initial bad feelings - sometimes achy feelings, tired, grouchy - it can vary. I keep focusing on the positive things that have changed for motivation, and then figure out which things I still need to change in order to keep my quest going. I really like understanding that the journey isn't linear - and that has certainly been my experience!

### magda — 2013-03-27T19:50:14+0200

I've seen the ASI mentioned a few times but what is it ? Will my doctor understand if I say that I would like ASI testing ? Also from reading the adrenal protocol I realize that I might be really low in progesterone and have estrogen dominance but Jack does not mention how he raised progesterone levels 'immediately' (considering natural methods). How can I naturally raise my progesterone levels and can stress and poor diet be a cause alone for low progesterone. I know I've always had issues with ovulation and upon quitting sugar and exercising I would ovulate instantly and get pregnant but otherwise my body wouldn't do it without these two steps (natural birth control ; ) .

Consults will end 4/21/2013.

# Thread: Can anyone help with recent salivary adrenocortex stress profile?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-anyone-help-with-recent-salivary-adrenocortex-stress-profile.5882/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Claudia — 2013-02-14T04:20:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-anyone-help-with-recent-salivary-adrenocortex-stress-profile.5882/post-75776>

Here are my results:

I had a recent salivary test of adrenocortex stress profile done this past January.
Morning is 11.3 (range is 6.0-42.0 nmol/L),
noon is 9.2 (2.0-11.0),
4 pm is 9.1 (2.0-11.0),
evening is 2.9 (1.0-8.0),
cortisol daily, total 32.5 (11.0-76.0),
DHEAS profile morning 13.9 (2.5-25.0 nmol/L) and
DHEAS/CORTISOL AM is 1.23 HIGH (0.20-0.60 RATIO)

I also have been taking t3 only for six months with some improvement in energy. I take 12.5mcg of t3 an hour or so before waking. If I don't wake up in time and take it at wake up, I don't feel as good. I had some weight gain when summer changed to fall and increased my t3 from 5 to 10 and then 12.5.

I don't think I'm LR but my hormones need balancing and I guess my adrenal test posted above is not normal. What do I do to improve my adrenals? I bought adrenal support by Gaia and it has ashwanganda and rhodiola and holy basil. I'm not sure if it's helping or not. The salivary test cost me $179, which is a LOT for me. I want to retest in 3-4 months but I am not sure if I will have the $ to do it. I want to avoid having to take hydrocortisone. My doc said that would be the next thing to try.

I welcome any advice, interpretation, ideas.

My diet is Paleo, not quite Epi...but heading there with shrimp and salmon several times a week, but mostly grass-fed beef, eggs, bacon, no grains for 4 years now, almost no fruit, lots of coconut oil, pastured raw butter (and not from A1 cows....the farm I buy from is A2 cows).

Thank you.

I no expert on adrenals but your morning cortisol is lowish but the rest for afternoon and night looks decent. It looks that you clock is off. I think you should try the BAB with morning sun gazing as per dr. K. This should help to turn of your melatonin in the morning. How is your sleep, Claudia?

### Claudia — 2013-02-25T19:20:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-anyone-help-with-recent-salivary-adrenocortex-stress-profile.5882/post-77599>

I think you should try the BAB with morning sun gazing as per dr. K. This should help to turn of your melatonin in the morning. How is your sleep, Claudia?

My sleep is good in the sense that I don't ever have insomnia or trouble falling asleep. I fall right to sleep, usually very tired, and I go to sleep too late...working on going to bed earlier. I do sleep in complete darkness. I wake up and urinate, once or twice per night, which I don't think is so good....is this related to adrenals. But I am also awakened by kids sometimes. I have 2, 10 years old and 5 years old. Thank you for your response Destiny!!!

### Patty Cakes — 2013-02-25T19:59:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-anyone-help-with-recent-salivary-adrenocortex-stress-profile.5882/post-77601>

Here are some cortisol guidelines:
Morning - Should be near the top of the range
Noon - Should be above middle but still closer to top of range
Afternoon - Close to middle of the range
Evening - Near the bottom of the range

Basically your afternoon one maybe a little high but I wouldn't worry about that one. Your morning one is definitely too low. Are you still doing the morning routine of looking at the sun upon waking and BAB? This will definitely help with morning cortisol production. Also look into Paul Robinson's book Recovering with T3. There is a method for taking T3 early in the morning to help stimulate your adrenals. But, as Jack states, adrenal dysfunction is a brain disease so I think this needs to be a multi-pronged approach.

I want to retest in 3-4 months but I am not sure if I will have the $ to do it. I want to avoid having to take hydrocortisone. My doc said that would be the next thing to try.
>
> I welcome any advice, interpretation, ideas.
>
> My diet is Paleo, not quite Epi...but heading there with shrimp and salmon several times a week, but mostly grass-fed beef, eggs, bacon, no grains for 4 years now, almost no fruit, lots of coconut oil, pastured raw butter (and not from A1 cows....the farm I buy from is A2 cows).
>
> Thank you.

I have been somewhat in your shoes and I can only tell you what helped me. I would force myself to wind down by 8pm and be in bed by 9pm because if I stayed up past 10pm I caught a second wind and could not sleep. After a while I started waking up with the sun and then I would get sun in my eyes and eat a BAB. Things have corrected over time but I still have to be mindful of this routine ALWAYS. Also, I wear my blue blockers and avoid EMF as much as possible at night.

It sounds like you are doing alot of things right. Sometimes it is the little things that make a big difference, I'm thinking light & EMF. Have you tried CT ing? Do you wake up refreshed or exhausted?

I wake up tired, never refreshed but not as exhausted as I had been. I do know about Paul Robinson and I have been doing the circadian dose. I take my one dose of t3 about 1.5-2 hours before waking. It's usually timed when the kids wake up normally and I hear them, wake up, take my t3, and go back to bed. I do think I feel better when I do this. A couple of times here and there I have missed the timing. It does seem to make a difference in my day. I don't take anymore t3 though, only one dose of 12.5mcg (one pill cut in half). I am honestly not into taking medications so this was hard for me as it is to take t3. I normally won't even take a Tylenol or Advil. I had some issues with hives so it was very slow going but I did notice good changes, etc. I did some swimming at the beach in April and May (which is early for New York). Then, I stopped for the summer or just swam at my beach every day here in New York. I was going to pick it up again in the fall. Actually I did continue until early October. I actually went to the beach today and walked barefoot but didn't go in the water. I though maybe I was doing some good grounding, earthing. I don't know a lot about this but both my son and I went barefoot (no brainer for him at 5 years old ) and it felt good. It was about 50 today and there was even some snow on the sand in some spots and I walked on the snow, too. We stayed barefoot for 30 minutes. I got lots of sunshine on my face, chest and arms.
I can't seem to do the bathtub...just have a great aversion to it this winter. I am planning on a polar bear plunge this Saturday. I prefer to just do the water at the beach, the bathtub is such a turnoff to me. It wasn't so bad last year when I first read about Dr. Kruse's CT.

PattyCakes, thanks for the cortisol guidelines. That's what I was thinking that the others are ok, but the morning is way too low. But if I do the morning light, isn't that too late to raise the cortisol? Or will it start helping with future mornings?

### Claudia — 2013-02-14T04:32:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenocortex-stress-profile.5883/post-75778>

SALIVA TEST 1/1/2013
Today, I got my adrenal saliva test results. The lab's comments: Saliva AM cortisol level is below mean range and suggests adrenal insufficiency. This suggests a degree of adrenal hypo function, maladaption/abnormal pacing with abnormal HPAA. Other cortisol levels are adepquate and within range. Evening is normal and within range.

Morning cortisol 11.3 (range 6.0-42.0 nmol/L)
midday, noon, 9.2 (2.0-11.0)
4pm, 9.1 (2.0-11.0)
evening, 8pm 2.9 (1.0-8.0)

Cortisol Daily, total 32.5 (11.0-76.0)

DHEAS Values
morning 13.9 (range 2.5-25.0)
DHEAS/Cortisol RATIO AM 1.23 HIGH (0.20-0.60 RATIO)

SALIVA DHEAS/CORTISOL RATIO An increase in this ratio is found in patients suffering from panic disorders. Suspect: An abnormal physiological response to stress, with shifting of the steroidogenic pathway to DHEA at the expense of cortisol.
Consider lifestyle changes: stress reduction
Herbal support: Adaptogenic herbs: American or Korean ginseng (Panax spp.), Siberian ginsend, WIthania, Licorice to prolong the half-life of cortisol, Sarsaparilla is a cortisol precursor
Glandular support: Adrenal, pituitary, others as indicated
Hormone replacement therapy: Cortisol, DHEA, pregnenlone as indicated
For herbal, glandular, and hormones, it is important to preserve or restore circadian rhythms by dosing in morning. May give 1/3 to 1/2 of morning dose at noon. Dosing later than noon is not advised. Consider measuring testosterone and or estradiol levels and intervene if necessary.

I'm not sure how bad this profile actually is. I've always felt tired at waking. I don't have problems feeling tired at night and falling asleep. Although I get up to pee a few times.

What can I do to improve my adrenals? What exactly is the adrenal RX and does it go hand in hand with leaky gut? Can someone help me with this? THank you so much.

I don't think I'm LR and I actually did do the Leptin Reset in Nov 2011 through January and then, I stopped when I had the signs. But my hormones need balancing, my t3 is low, and I"m taking t3 only (low dose of 12.5 mcg per day), and Jack has told me that I have mitochondrial inefficiency. I have off and on fatigue, moodiness toward depression, weight gain (for what feels like no reason at all and unrelated to what I eat).

I don't do the BAB because I'm just not hungry. In fact, I'm often not hungry. So, I've been practicing intuitive eating and paying attention to my body and hunger signals. If I am not hungry in the morning, and my cortisol is on the lower side, then, am I right to think this is not a good time for me to eat A LOT of food? It would be forcing since I am not hungry. I get a little hungry in the afternoon and then, I eat. I am hungry again at dinner, and I eat. I don't get very hungry ever. I think when I do eat the combination of protein and fat keeps me satiated for many hours.

Thanks for any advice, interpretation. I posted in some other threads, too. I wasn't sure where to post .

### janagram — 2013-02-14T19:03:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-if-you-shouldnt-take-sulphur.5886/post-75833>

How do you know if you shouldn't take sulphur? A naturopath that I only saw twice suggested that maybe I should avoid sulphur but I cannot remember why? Any symptoms to clue us? Or is it all some test?
(I have started taking Sam-e.....)

### Rachelle — 2013-02-15T19:17:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-if-you-shouldnt-take-sulphur.5886/post-75991>

sulphur in homeopathic form or what?

### DarleenMB — 2013-02-15T19:43:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-if-you-shouldnt-take-sulphur.5886/post-75992>

If it were me, I'd want a solid reason why. I'd go back and ask for further clarification.

Sulphur is found in many foods such as eggs, broccoli, meat, etc. It's also in MSM which I've been taking but have now stopped since I got my 23andme results.

### freesia — 2013-02-15T19:58:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-if-you-shouldnt-take-sulphur.5886/post-75996>

Some people have problems making sulfate out of sulfur (related to the SUOX enzyme reaction IIRC) and sometimes people with that problem can have problems with excess sulfites as a result. This can apparently, impact glutathione levels negatively. You can google sulfite sensitivity and see if you think it might apply to you maybe?

I wouldn't avoid sulfur without a very good, clear reason either.

### springs — 2013-02-16T02:03:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-if-you-shouldnt-take-sulphur.5886/post-76041>

You can order sulfite and sulfate strips to test your urine, if you are concerned. Dr. Yasko offers them, but they're cheaper at CTL Scientific. The CBS mutations will also cause excess sulfite/sulfate. You can also have nutritional deficiencies, like with molybdenum, that will keep sulfites from being turned into sulfates.

### janagram — 2013-02-16T08:03:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-if-you-shouldnt-take-sulphur.5886/post-76090>

sooooo...Im also taking sam-e....which is a form of sulfur....hmmmm. then I read that it can prevent glutathione production....I just don't know....gonna go look up some stuff.

### nonchalant — 2013-02-18T16:10:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-5-is-live-get-ready-for-some-windex-on-your-glass-eyes.5887/post-76314>

> [bigknitwit said:](/goto/post?id=76053)
>
> Another energy production mechanism that doesn't involve the mitochondria? Is this is ellusive way in which we can make energy without needing T3?

Our cell membranes produce an electric charge, using sodium/potassium ion pumps. Both ions are positively charged, but the cell pumps out 3 sodium ions and pumps in 2 potassium ions at a time, which results in a net negative charge. The membrane also has leak channels which selectively allow potassium to leak out due to diffusion, but keep sodium out. The pumps use ATP for energy, the leak channels are passive. The cell membrane is an extremely thin insulator, which makes for extremely high capacitance levels between the 2 conductors (the ionic solution inside and outside the cell.)

Hmm, maybe I'm ready for my Bioelectricity 101 test now?

> Our cell membranes produce an electric charge, using sodium/potassium ion pumps. Both ions are positively charged, but the cell pumps out 3 sodium ions and pumps in 2 potassium ions at a time, which results in a net negative charge. The membrane also has leak channels which selectively allow potassium to leak out due to diffusion, but keep sodium out. The pumps use ATP for energy, the leak channels are passive. The cell membrane is an extremely thin insulator, which makes for extremely high capacitance levels between the 2 conductors (the ionic solution inside and outside the cell.)
>
> Hmm, maybe I'm ready for my Bioelectricity 101 test now?

Everything in here is wrong........EMF 7 shows you why.

> [bigknitwit said:](/goto/post?id=76386)

>

Are they not just given a little vibrational bump - an energy boost that makes them move a little faster? Or is there some quantum effect here (in the energy transfer) that you are elluding to that is not-so-nice?

A cell is not a machine. It is alive and quite dynamic. Each particle in it has its own field it reacts to in the environment. If that magnetic field changes, and ours has in a big way it disorders the order that life creates and a shit storm ensues. What do we find in a living organism you ask? There is organized heterogeneity in life, or dynamic structures on all scales. There is no homogeneity, no static phase held at any level. Even a single cell has its characteristic shape and anatomy, all parts of which are in constant motion (Brownian movement due to water); its electrical potentials and mechanical properties similarly, are subject to cyclic and non-cyclic changes as it responds to, and counteracts environmental fluctuations, like the magnetic field it finds itself in. This determines how well it works. Life is set to work in a set field of harmonic resonance (schumann) to work optimally. This is where a symphony is created from the chaos of chemicals in a cell. So a magnetic field is like a conductor of sorts that sets the tone for how things are done in a cell.
Spatially, the cell is partitioned into numerous compartments by cellular membrane stacks and organelles, each with its own ‘steady state’ of processes that can respond directly to external stimuli and relay signals to other compartments of the cell. Also subject the magnetic field.

With cellular compartments, microdomains can be separately energized to give local circuits, and complexes of two or more molecules can function as ‘molecular energy machines’ by themselves that can cycle autonomously without immediate reference to its surroundings. In other words, the steady ‘state’ is not a state at all but a conglomerate of processes spatiotemporally organised, i.e., it has a deep space-time structure, and cannot be represented as an instantaneous state, or even a configuration of states. This space time structure is subject to precise quantum time. When it is off you get sick and you could die faster than you think. This is where we are today and laid out in EMF-6. Characteristic times of processes range from <10−14 s for resonant energy transfer between molecules to 107 s for circannual rhythms. The spatial extent of processes, similarly, spans at least ten orders of magnitude from 10−10 m for intramolecular interactions to metres for nerve conduction and the general coordination of movements in larger animals like us humans.
The processes are catenated in both **time and space**: the extremely rapid transient flows (very short-lived pulses of chemicals or of energy), which take place on receiving specific signals, are propagated to longer and longer time domains of minutes, hours, days, and so on via interlocking processes that ultimately straddle generations. this is where the quantum dance of epigenetics takes place and if timing is off molecular chaos is the result. Understanding magnetic fields is the domain of quantum biology.

The pathway uses very little energy from ATP and gives a whole lot to the organism who uses it. But it requires cold to be present. In the pathway, the less effort you give, the faster and more powerful you will be when this pathway is active. People who live in this pathway can run a marathon with no training. They can lift unreal amounts of weight with little training. Their reserve and recovery are just incredible. You have to see it to believe it. When we have extinction events the events usually affect the evaporation of water in some fashion from the surface of lakes and oceans. It also affects the transpiration from the forrest trees, plants, and flowers and this change cools the air. You must understand how climatology works here; liquid water needs to absorb a lot of latent heat to in order to evaporate, so it sucks energy from the atmosphere to make this transfer. This loss of energy from the atmosphere directly cools the planet and this preserves the charge on lifeâ€™s inner mitochondrial membrane and in the nanotubes present in our cells that contain water. This is how life lives long in the cold.

# Thread: I just wanted to say.....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-just-wanted-to-say.5895/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### caroline — 2013-02-15T01:50:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-just-wanted-to-say.5895/post-75928>

Since this is Valentines day - a day to appreciate others in your life ....I just wanted to say thanks to everyone who posts. You never know when something you say sticks to someone else. I have found this over and over and over again. Something that someone else says becomes actionable for you in your life that you wouldn't have considered before. Lots of times - it takes awhile and then you remember something that becomes really relevant to you ...at least that is what happens for me.
When I weighed myself this morning I remembered something that Destiny had said quite awhile ago now and I had taken it on board after I had thought about it for awhile. I think this is how it goes - Dr. K had said that she needed to stop eating fruit and she would lose the excess weight effortlessly - of course there were other reasons because of Marta's situation \[I hope I am remembering that right? apologies if I am not\]

Anyway - I decided to stop eating fruit for awhile \[I love,love.love fruit\] I have effortlessly dropped 2 Kilos and am eating like a horse \[I am embarrassed to say!\] I can't believe how much I eat and my weight is very stable and going down .....but the very stable part is what is great. I used to always feel like I could gain at the blink of an eye. Always scared to get on the scale but I knew that I had to. But there has been so many other things that have made a huge difference for me - so thanks again!

So- what you share is hugely important to all of us ...just as JK is sharing with us ....so we need to pay if forward......

### SeaHorse — 2013-02-15T04:17:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-just-wanted-to-say.5895/post-75946>

Great point Caroline...I too have taken things I've read here and have had them come to life for me in ways I didn't expect. All the experimentation, opinions, experiences and discourse are the living embodiment of change....and that's what we are all doing.

Happy Valentines to you too!

### Stephen — 2013-02-15T12:40:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-31-year-old-male.5899/post-75958>

Any thoughts on my below labs would be greatly appreciated.

As background I'm a 31 year old male, 188cm (6' 2") tall, 80kg (176lbs), and carrying body fat of somewhere under 10%. I've been experimenting with Jack's ideas for quite a while now (at least two winters) with what I feel have been very good results.

Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate (ESR): 1mm/hr (1 - 20)

**Full Blood Count**
Hb: 140 g/L (130 - 180)
Total WCC: 4.0 10^9/L (4.0 - 11.0)
RCC: 5.2 10^12/L (4.5 - 6.5)
Hct: 0.44 g/L (0.38 - 0.54)
MCV: 85 fL (80 - 97)
MCH: 29 pg (27 - 32)
MCHC: 336 g/L (320 - 360)
Plt: 193 10^9/L (150 - 450)
Neutrophils: 52% and 2.1 (2.0 - 8.0)
Lymphocytes: 40% and 1.6 (1.0 - 4.0)
Monocytes: 6% and 0.2 (0.0 - 1.0)
Eosinophils: 2% and 0.1 (0.0 - 0.5)
Basophils: 1% and 0.0 (0.0 - 0.3)

Serum Creatinin: 100 umol/L (50 - 120)
eGFR: 76 mL/min/1.73 m2

Fasting Glucose: 5.0 mmol/L (3.0 - 5.5)

Cholesterol: 4.9 mmol/L (3.1 - 5.5)
Triglycerides: 0.5 mmol/L (0.5 - 2.0)
HDL: 2.1 mmol/L (>1.0)
LDL: 2.6 mmol/L (0.0 - 3.5)
CRF (Chol/HDL Ratio): 2.3 (<5.0)

Sodium: 137 mmol/L (135 - 145)
Potassium: 4.9 mmol/L (3.5 - 5.5)
Chloride: 103 mmol/L (95 - 110)
Bicarbonate: 23 mmol/L (19 - 32)
Urea: 8.5 mmol/L (2.5 - 8.0)
Uric acid: 0.36 mmol/L (0.2 - 0.46)
T. Bilirubin: 22 umol/L (1 - 30)
Protein (Total) 69 g/L (62 - 82)
Albumin: 45 g/L (35 - 50)
Globulin 24 g/L (23 - 38)
Alk Phos: 65 U/L (0 - 135)
GGT: 15 U/L (0 - 50)
ALT: 34 U/L (0 - 40)
AST: 25 U/L (0 - 45)
Lactate Dehydro: 120 U/L (120 - 260)
Calcium (Total): 2.39 mmol/L (2.10 - 2.60)
Calcium (Adjusted): 2.29 mmol/L (2.10 - 2.60)
Phosphate (Inorg: 1.29 mmol/L (0.8 - 1.60)

Magnesium: 0.86 mmol/L (0.7 - 1.10)

hsCRP: 0.1 (<0.1 - 8.0)

Iron: 19.1 umol/L (9.0 - 30.0)
Transferrin: 2.2 g/L (2.0 - 3.7)
Total Iron Bind: 51 umol/L (45 - 80)
Saturation: 37.5% (16.0 - 60.0)
Ferritin: 159 ug/L (30 - 300)

Serum Zinc: 12 umol/L (10 - 16)

TSH: 0.76 mIU/L (0.40 - 3.80)

25 OH Vitamin D: 179.5 nmol/L (25.0 - 139.0)

<25 Deficient
25 - 74 Insufficient
75 - 250 Sufficient
\>250 Potential intoxication

Testosterone (Total): 13.58 nmol/L (7.00 - 30.00)
SHBG: 22.4 nmol/L (11.00 - 78.00)
Testosterone Free Calculated: 329 pmol/L (120 - 470)

# Thread: small win!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 42
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### KiwiLauren — 2013-02-16T02:48:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76055>

We are winding down our summer here. Before summer began Dr Kruse discussed with me the idea of doing an n-1 test re: my ability to synthesise vitamin D without supplementation. I have done semi-naked grounded sunbathing for at least one hour per day over summer. In addition to being very tan for the first time in my life, my levels are now higher than when I was supplementing. (I still probably need to supplement to get firmly into the optimal range, but according to the NZ lab levels I'm over the top!). Hurray. This is a small win, but seems a really positive sign.

### Shijin13 — 2013-02-16T02:49:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76056>

Yeah Lauren...awesome.... its the little things that make us feel awesome...

### fitness@home — 2013-02-16T03:17:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76060>

> [KiwiLauren said:](/goto/post?id=76055)
>
> We are winding down our summer here. Before summer began Dr Kruse discussed with me the idea of doing an n-1 test re: my ability to synthesise vitamin D without supplementation. I have done semi-naked grounded sunbathing for at least one hour per day over summer. In addition to being very tan for the first time in my life, my levels are now higher than when I was supplementing. (I still probably need to supplement to get firmly into the optimal range, but according to the NZ lab levels I'm over the top!). Hurray. This is a small win, but seems a really positive sign.

This is great Lauren! I'd like to duplicate your experiment during our summer. Unfortunately, the peak sun time is while I'm at work. I don't think the parents at the park I frequent for my lunch hour each day would appreciate it If I sunbathed semi-naked.

Wonder how many days per week would produce measurable results? I have weekends and every other Friday off.

### KiwiLauren — 2013-02-16T03:25:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76061>

> [fitness@home said:](/goto/post?id=76060)
>
> This is great Lauren! I'd like to duplicate your experiment during our summer. Unfortunately, the peak sun time is while I'm at work. I don't think the parents at the park I frequent for my lunch hour each day would appreciate it If I sunbathed semi-naked.
>
> Wonder how many days per week would produce measurable results? I have weekends and every other Friday off.

LOL. I wonder if you could get away with a tank top and shorts at the park during lunch hours? And then do longer on weekends and your Fridays off. My yard has very little privacy and while I mostly work from home I did have to wear something most days. I think if you could get 80% of your body exposed that would be enough. It was a really great experiment to do, though, to understand how my body is functioning at the moment. (By the way, if you try it, the way we talked about was to re-test Vitamin D with about a month of summer left to go - that way if your levels are falling/not sustaining themselves, you still have ample time to supplement and get them higher before winter.)

### bigknitwit — 2013-02-16T15:15:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76104>

Love this! Would you care to share your before (summer)/now lab numbers?

### BJK77 — 2013-02-16T16:34:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76110>

That's great Lauren! Isn't it such an empowering feeling to be able to take control of your own health? Love it!

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-16T17:11:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76112>

### diane — 2013-02-16T17:13:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76113>

That's great Lauren! Did you go out whether it was sunny or not? Do we still get benefit from the vitamin D on cloudy days, but perhaps at lower levels?

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-16T17:17:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76114>

Vit D production varies with time of day, time of year, latitude, cloudiness, clothes, haze, ozone, etc. Clouds block the rays that produce D more or less, depending on thickness & totality of coverage.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-16T17:55:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76116>

not exactly true when quantum time is off.......

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-16T18:47:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76119>

Sure, there's the possibility that one's body can't make D even in the best of circumstances. That's why I asked the question, was she expecting she \*couldn't\* make D?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2013-02-16T19:06:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76122>

> [KiwiLauren said:](/goto/post?id=76055)
>
> We are winding down our summer here. Before summer began Dr Kruse discussed with me the idea of doing an n-1 test re: my ability to synthesise vitamin D without supplementation. I have done semi-naked grounded sunbathing for at least one hour per day over summer. In addition to being very tan for the first time in my life, my levels are now higher than when I was supplementing. (I still probably need to supplement to get firmly into the optimal range, but according to the NZ lab levels I'm over the top!). Hurray. This is a small win, but seems a really positive sign.

Yippeeee! Love it! Go full monte, broadcast, and then you can afford even more labs!!!

### KiwiLauren — 2013-02-16T21:01:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76137>

> [MamaGrok said:](/goto/post?id=76119)
>
> Sure, there's the possibility that one's body can't make D even in the best of circumstances. That's why I asked the question, was she expecting she \*couldn't\* make D?

MG, there seems to be some suggestion that some people cannot make vitamin D on their own. It's way out of my intellectual league, but even my doctor said to me that I might be one of those people. Then of course there is the idea that my current state is/was too 'broken' to do this properly. For me, a year + ago, I wouldn't have lasted 10 minutes without sunburn. I had to avoid peak sun times. This alone makes it pretty damn hard to get sufficient vitamin D. So there were multiple factors I was ruling out. And I was pleased to say that I can now eliminate them all.

BKW, I'm still trying to make sure I've accurately converted my UK-system numbers to the US-system and I'm not sure I have. What I can tell you for certain is that prior to stopping supplementation, I was in the top 1/3 of the New Zealand range for vitamin D. For the past 4 months I have had no supplementation at all, regardless of weather. (I did get outside on cloudy days; I didn't if it was pouring rain. I always tried to be outside at the hottest time of the day. I usually got more than 1 hour a day because I've been trying to work from home outside, too.). My current results are at the very top of the range. (But, take this with a grain of salt because my initial attempts at converting appear to show that the top of the NZ range is bottom of the optimal US one.)

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76150>

That really is awesome! I have definitely improved my sun tolerance in the last few years, but I have no idea which thing did it. And I would certainly like to improve it much more. What do you think was the big key (or keys) for you?

### Dextery — 2013-03-03T20:49:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-78779>

Your 124 nmol/L is around 50ng/ml We need 70 to 100ng/ml but your 50 is great. Just more sun is needed.

### Inger — 2013-03-05T08:15:35+0200

This just made me so happy...!!!

My N=1 about tanning naked is, it is a amazing tool for healing... close to magic! I bet when tanning the "secret" parts it boost our hormones big time and then through that everything gets better and vit D is better assimilated too.. or something like that.
I know it was used against cancer too in the good old days. Dr Nolfi, a Danish Doctor that also had a natural healing hospital named HumlegÃ¥rden got breast cancer and healed it with raw foods and tanning naked 3 hours out on the grass every day. Completely naked. A bikini would not do the same at all. She believed vegetarian raw foods was the way to go so I bet it was the tanning naked that got her healthy.. On the earth. The earthing at the same time must be a huge factor too..
Imagine what results will we get eating raw oysters and tanning naked too... huh! It can be only magic!

AFAIK light skin absorbs vit D from the sun far better than dark skin does, so if we are dark we need way more sun to get the same effect... not a good idea to be black and live in Scandinavia unless you really take caution.

The other day the sun was out and temps around freezing.. and I tanned naked 3 hours on my balcony. I had my earthing strap on too so I was connected to the waterfall.. Wow it was good...! Sure from winter sun you want make any vit D but I know it has huge benefits anyways, I can feel it. It must be something with the light and the sunbeams..

### caroline — 2013-03-05T09:42:55+0200

### MamaGrok — 2013-03-05T15:57:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-79076>

Sunning causes D to rise, and sunning causes tan (in most), but tan does not cause D to rise.

Inger, I'm curious about the completely naked tanning. Every society I've ever heard or read about, women covered their genitals, even in the hottest regions. Nature itself covers them with location & hair. Almost all men covered genitals, as well, but there were a handful that didn't. Obviously, the earliest humans, many hundreds of thousands of years ago, wouldn't have been covered other than by what nature provided.

MamaGrok,
I cover my genitals too.. when with others...lol. Might well have been that they back then sunbathed naked too, alone? I have no clue.. I just know it feels amazing! (remember you need to let the sun beams "right there"..) Way different than tanning other parts of the body.
Maybe also back then they was living in such a sync with nature and all time grounded that it was not that needed either because their hormones were pretty optimal? Women probably did not covered their breasts, that is a pretty modern thing I guess. Especially the nipples on the breasts, as far as I have experienced, loves sunshine. I can feel it physically, a slight feeling but it is there.
Have you tried the naked tanning MamaGrok? If, how did it felt for you?

### ruby — 2013-03-08T11:56:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-79626>

> [MamaGrok said:](/goto/post?id=79076)

> Nature itself covers them with location & hair.

According to Desmond Morris Nature uses pubic hair as a visual signal that a person is now a sexual adult ready to breed. It's meant to trigger a sexual response, while its absence inhibited it. Pubic hair also acts as a scent trap facilitating pheromone signalling (there's more to it and he elaborates quite a bit in his books  He does not say anything about black absorbing more solar energy but it may play a role...

### MamaGrok — 2013-03-08T15:42:25+0200

Of course it has multiple purposes, but the reality is that it also blocks light from women's genitals.

Yes, I thought I mentioned it above, but in the societies with the least clothing, men & women wore no covering on their upper bodies, men usually covered genitals, but didn't always, and women always covered genitals, in the "primitive" societies that I have researched, both ancient and modern. This was all pre-missionary, b/c of course, many missionaries liked to cover them shoulders to knees, and in some cases, wrists to ankles!

### Inger — 2013-03-08T15:46:27+0200

### BJK77 — 2013-02-16T14:38:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-pop-quiz.5912/post-76098>

Suppose you only have 15-20 minutes to devote to CT on a very busy day. Do you choose to:

A) Hope in a 50-degree cold tub for 20 minutes; or
B) Spend 10-15 minutes outside in 32 degree weather playing in the snow!

I'm opting for B just to soak up the electrons along with the benefits of the cold, but curious what others think since there would be less time outside and it's definitely not full submersion.

### fitness@home — 2013-02-16T14:39:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-pop-quiz.5912/post-76099>

B!

Outside with some grounding sounds much better...

### Shijin13 — 2013-02-16T15:32:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-pop-quiz.5912/post-76105>

B.... but then w/snow I'd be in it a lot longer than 15 mins....

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-16T15:37:24+0200

# Thread: TSH plummeting majorly....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tsh-plummeting-majorly.5917/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope — 2013-02-16T22:20:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tsh-plummeting-majorly.5917/post-76162>

So I got some new labs...and though sadly my FT3/4 is still in the tank (2.6 and 1, respectively), my TSH has tanked and gone down to 0.02. I have been having bad heart pounding/racing so that I had to stop my thyroid meds and some of my sea veggies to not have this happen a lot, especially at night.

Has anyone seen this?

I wonder if I am not able to absorb selenium.....and if that contributes to anything.

The crazy/good thing is that I also lost about 10 lbs since late Nov. and have started generally to feel better....more energy, better brain, from all the stuff I have been doing from the Kruse Rx including more meditation and some light yoga now and then.

But this is a bummer.....especially because I feel I need the thyroid med (Armour, half grain) to up the FT3/4....eventually.

Has anyone seen this happen? Any other insight you could share??

Thanks in advance...

# Thread: Women's experiences with CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/womens-experiences-with-ct.5918/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### megzy9281@yahoo.com — 2013-02-16T23:22:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/womens-experiences-with-ct.5918/post-76163>

Hi there, I'll be starting CT again for my fatigue issues and am motivated to stick with it this time. I'd like to hear experiences from other women (especially those with adrenal fatigue) who got thru all the discomfort and detox and made it to finally be cold adapted. Hearing from other's struggles turned successes would be good motivation for me!! Thank you!

### freesia — 2013-02-17T04:25:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/womens-experiences-with-ct.5918/post-76183>

Hi,
I started with a basal body temperature average of 95.9 F and maximum afternoon temperature of 97.5 F - often lower.
10 months later - CTing every one or two days - I have an average basal body temp of 97.4 F and afternoon temperature of 98.6 F.

I got down from 20 minutes in a 60 F bath to 45 minutes in a 46 F bath over the course of about 12 weeks. Took it slowly (like a sick person, not a sportsperson) but was _very_ consistent.

Overcame discomfort. Check! Check!  You don't really want me to dwell on that bit do you?
Detox was a strange one - mostly estrogen I think. Wow..I remember getting pimples, and mood swings LOL. DIM and I-3-C saved the day!
For quite a while I used to wake up the next morning feeling slammed by the day before's bath. CT hangover...
I managed that by ice bathing every second day instead of daily until I could push it again.

I forget how many weeks in (but not too many) my body started heating up in anticipation of a bath, and I could stretch the time out to 45 minutes, from 20 - 30 minutes. In Winter when we were walking in an icy wind in the city one of my children mentioned my hands were so warm. I was always someone who had cold hands and feet and HATED Winter...feared the cold.

Particular adrenal symptoms I had were a crash mid afternoon, and waking around 3am a bit panicky. Leptin reset and CT together seemed to sort this out quickly.
Morning and evening cortisol levels were in normal range 4 months in.

Hope that helps.

### megzy9281@yahoo.com — 2013-02-18T03:42:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/womens-experiences-with-ct.5918/post-76296>

Thank you so much for telling me your experience. Super helpful. Back to it, 2nd day of face dunks... Here we go!!


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: One year anniversary for CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-year-anniversary-for-ct.5920/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dali Dula — 2013-02-17T16:07:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-year-anniversary-for-ct.5920/post-76199>

So it's about a year since many of us started the CT protocol. How you all doing? Thoughts, comments, results, transformations? I have had a great time with CT. Truly life changing.
My thyroid function,indicated mostly by normal body temp and energy levels, has improved. I still take synthroid, but dosage has been reduced.
My labs for the most part are not drastically improved, But I feel great.
All of my physical functions have improved; digestion, sexual, brain fog.
Did I mention I feel great? My body comp, energy level, strength, and speed are all greatly improved. I can run really fast.
I love the fact that I can now swim anywhere unless the water is frozen. Playing in the surf in the cold is a blast, it makes me laugh.
I have enjoyed outdoor winter activities more than ever. Hiking and skiing in a t shirt or shirtless in temps I never would have thought possible before.
I now embrace the cold instead of hiding from it.
My weight has not changed for over 8 months 5'9" 165 lbs and I still continue to improve body comp. No weight change, just trading muscle for fat.
I shiver much less than I did at the beginning.
I love the CT high. Endocannabinoids at play.
Did I mention I feel great?

Let us know how this is working for you. Happy Anniversary!

### Shijin13 — 2013-02-17T16:35:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-year-anniversary-for-ct.5920/post-76203>

I'm loving CT When I do it... Though sometimes its a challenge w/my Pg and Thyroid tanked... but I keep pushing forward... its the one thing that's saving me... a friend is giving us her xtra deep freezer - we're going to use it specifically for making ice...

### PrimalPam — 2013-02-17T16:43:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-year-anniversary-for-ct.5920/post-76204>

I've come to love my time in the tub. I leave my Ipod docking station and thermometer in the bathroom all the time now, so I have no excuses. Just throw my ice in, fill the tub, and get in. I've created a bunch of playlists so I can just lay there until my time is up. Recently, I've bought some ice pack things so I can spot ice while hanging around the house, and I'm finding it's just not cold enough!

I confess that I skipped about 4 months of CT and just recently got back in the tub. I had ice, frozen in plastic containers all over the freezer, for 4 straight months. Don't ask me why I stopped, I just did. Recently, I got back in, and it's like an addiction, where I want to get in every day. The good news is that, after 4 months off, getting back in was a breeze. I was afraid of that "new CT'er learning curve", but it was super easy.

Funny, but when I first started CT last year, I did 5x/week and had no detox symptoms. But after 4 months off (and some holiday non-EP eating), I broke out all over my face. Serves me right.

So, the reason I decided to get my big a\*\* back in? My FBG had gone up. After 4 months of being out of the tub, FBG was in the 115-120 range. Yikes. It had gotten down to the high 80's/low 90's, and here I was, playing with fire. So I'm going to wait a few weeks and take it again. Intellectually, I KNOW CT lowers FBG, so why did I think I was going to keep it down without it??

### SeaHorse — 2013-02-17T18:02:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-year-anniversary-for-ct.5920/post-76211>

I think CT has been the backbone for most of the changes I've experienced this year. I love the feeling that I get while I'm in the water and afterwards. I've gained all kinds of muscle that certainly hasn't appeared because of exercise, I like the way my body looks leaner and more defined (new experience!), I know that CT will brighten my day mentally which is a big plus for me, I've liked the challenge of figuring out how I can CT outside without hurting my hands and feet (Reynauds), I've learned to find the sweet spot that comes with the right temp. and the right amount of time...and how that changes through the seasons. I'm officially in love with our pond!

I think I'm going to be one of those people who will continue to see changes over a really long period of time particularly in the hormone department, but I'm in for the long haul and CT is just part of my life now

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-17T18:06:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-year-anniversary-for-ct.5920/post-76212>

soon you'll see why you feel that way

### Dali Dula — 2013-02-17T18:10:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-year-anniversary-for-ct.5920/post-76215>

> [SeaHorse said:](/goto/post?id=76211)
>
> I think CT has been the backbone for most of the changes I've experienced this year. I love the feeling that I get while I'm in the water and afterwards. I've gained all kinds of muscle that certainly hasn't appeared because of exercise, I like the way my body looks leaner and more defined (new experience!), I know that CT will brighten my day mentally which is a big plus for me, I've liked the challenge of figuring out how I can CT outside without hurting my hands and feet (Reynauds), I've learned to find the sweet spot that comes with the right temp. and the right amount of time...and how that changes through the seasons. I'm officially in love with our pond!
>
> I think I'm going to be one of those people who will continue to see changes over a really long period of time particularly in the hormone department, but I'm in for the long haul and CT is just part of my life now

I am in love with my neighbor's pond. ( Thou shall not covet thy neighbor's pond). Doing CT through the seasons in a wilderness pond gives one a deep appreciation of the world.

### caroline — 2013-02-18T01:04:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-year-anniversary-for-ct.5920/post-76274>

I am thrilled for all the amazing results you guys are having and your dedication. What a massive change in a year physically and most important mentally for all of us.

I go in the ocean everyday and can't be without it - it is like something very, very important is missing in my day - so - no matter how late - I still do it. It isn't cold enough and I can't get my bath tub cold enough either so I am hanging out for winter!

# Thread: D'Adamo's Blood Type Testing and Diet
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dadamos-blood-type-testing-and-diet.5921/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Martin — 2013-02-17T17:27:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dadamos-blood-type-testing-and-diet.5921/post-76207>

One of my docs in the past wanted me to get tested for my blood type and then base a diet on the results. Dw and I then had a dark field microscopy test. Much to our surprise, my blood cells were happily floating and bumping and freely moving about while DW's were all clump-assed together. He suggested that we follow **The Blood Type Diet** and offered up this book on the subject.

I complied, read Peter D'Adamo's book and changed some foods. No coconut!

No help. Moved on.

I ran across a review on the Weston Price site that really confirmed changing my mind back to traditional foods, and, not to eliminate nor add things based on D'Adamo's "theories". I went on to eat more and more traditional foods according to Price and others.

One guy, one book, one theory. I learned that what works in vitro doesn't always equate to what works in vivo.

Big lesson.

[http://www.westonaprice.org/thumbs-down-reviews/eat-right-4-your-type](http://www.westonaprice.org/thumbs-down-reviews/eat-right-4-your-type)

Lots of interesting examinations of D'Adamo's flawed theories in the above link.

### janagram — 2013-02-28T07:28:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dadamos-blood-type-testing-and-diet.5921/post-78261>

As an O, I learned I needed meat and lots of it....really helped me since I had been a vegetarian for decades....(!) But I allowed as how I could probably eat coconut oil and be fine...and have now embraced epi-paleo.

### Destiny — 2013-03-01T13:20:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dadamos-blood-type-testing-and-diet.5921/post-78486>

### Martha Ray — 2019-05-28T02:49:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dadamos-blood-type-testing-and-diet.5921/post-271412>

I believe that both of the Dr. D'Adamo's added value and that the Blood Type Diet is good for many people as it stresses real food and lowers enough lectins for each Blood Type. The food lists for O and A are good not perfect. They are the only Blood Types I have experience with. I've given them a hearing and a good long trial and have noticed the trends true to myself as well as most of my family and friends that have tried it. It is just a tiny step toward individualizing dieting. The fact that we are different blood types and some are secretors and some nonsecretors is too unique to not factor in somehow... just as our own genetics factor. Much more to learn I believe. I believe that plant 'based' especially green leafy's has been reasonable food over the ages. All food is under the gun quality wise depending on how much man has tinkered 'improving' it... ha. The power of the mind to have confidence in what one decides to ingest is most likely a huge factor as well. Growing up eating the SAD that I perceived as 'healthy', then natural hygiene, zero carb, macrobiotic, carb and carb and sugar addict, vegetarian, vegan, paleo, alkaline paleo, Weston Price, blood type diets, ad nauseam.... I was pretty much a professional dieter. Some things aligned and I've failed enough to be open to what I don't know what I don't know.
I love Jack Kruse and within the past 4 months been enjoying the fresh air...I like outside the box thinkers... and I am extremely inquisitive. I've got a lot to learn.

Growing up eating the SAD that I perceived as 'healthy', then natural hygiene, zero carb, macrobiotic, carb and carb and sugar addict, vegetarian, vegan, paleo, alkaline paleo, Weston Price, blood type diets, ad nauseam.... I was pretty much a professional dieter. Some things aligned and I've failed enough to be open to what I don't know what I don't know.

Hi Martha

Have you read the D'Adamo book The Genotype diet? Adds biometric measurements to the mix, giving an indication of how the womb environment influences the genes, and how the environment we subsequently face can turn up the volume. From my biometrics information plus my blood type I learned that I am more than typically primed to survive famine, wish I'd known that before I started on the chronic dieting road.  It was definitely more useful to me than the blood type diet, changed my relationship with food, and knowing my risks based on my biometrics, it has influenced my IF practice.

### Martha Ray — 2019-12-05T19:42:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dadamos-blood-type-testing-and-diet.5921/post-283145>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=271430)
>
> Hi Martha
>
> Have you read the D'Adamo book The Genotype diet? ...

My post was not allowed just got blocked on a Facebook BTD site... but I think the word needs to get out... that we all need to learn from each other.....

**[Martha Ray Barger](https://www.facebook.com/martha.barger?fref=gs&__tn__=%2CdlC-R-R&eid=ARCsAS_qhHCUqWO6MIdiW6MnifLpAz2HD8cOj5WC-76idr-23BXsyZqEnruUXA_WX1JCR0DEZIympp-Y&hc_ref=ARTP6j4dsQkDctic1hKpI9e8-wIKXxSyL51iRzxrJ-ttdmh6vxFjsTrKydNhdgLWtD8&dti=396351555195&hc_location=group)** shared a [link](https://www.facebook.com/groups/396351555195/permalink/10157453986945196/).

[Just now](https://www.facebook.com/groups/396351555195/permalink/10157453986945196/)

Thinking of getting a new dog...I feel dogs do best with a meat based diet but just wonder if the D'Adamo's had any opinion on dogs who have ..."**Dogs have** more than 12 **blood groups**, and their red **blood** cells may contain any combination of these since each **blood** group is inherited independently. The most important of these is called **Dog** Erythrocyte Antigen (DEA) 1.1. Typing of **blood** donors and recipients is done before transfusion."
