### Raiken3712 — 2013-02-17T21:55:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-adaption-time.5923/post-76240>

So I've been doing CT for a solid week and don't really notice any difference. I know 2 weeks is the given time for adaption and that may vary, but I'm curious as to any signs I can look for to know whether I'm improving.

For those that have successfully adapted....radiating heat and all the various improvements..did you notice it all at once? Or did you notice that you were radiating heat one day and than a few days down the road it increased? Is there some progression here? Or for some people did it just seem to happen all at once. Also what were the list of benefits. Maybe some happened progressively while some just seemed to show up all at once?

For example did it seem like nothing was going on for 13 days and day 14 bam radiating heat etc...? Also for some reason I'm wondering if eating carbohydrates to a great degree could be slowing me down. I'll reread the CT series...but I do find it interesting the amount of time it takes to adapt to fat and get into good Ketosis is 2 weeks...the same amount of time for CT.

Is this related at all...is this part of the reason high fat meals go with CT? Do you need to be running on Ketones at least partially to adapt? I've been struggling with binge eat well cycles..where I binge on carbohydrates and than eat well from 1-4 days and than binge again.

Am I making it impossible to adapt to the cold by binging? Do I need a full two weeks of high fat and relatively low carb for this to work? Has anyone adapted while eating larger amounts of carbohydrates?

Thanks as always...really hope I can get past this binge issue...I'm worried I'm wrecking my body..but so many things set me off. I can't seem to eat anything with sugar including Fruit...and than I binge. Do I need to just stay away from anything with carbohydrates for a good long time to get rid of this?

lkajsdlkfffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffassssssssssssssssssfjalksjdfalksdjf

So frustrating...I hate the cold. It just seems to last forever sitting in cold water....the cold isn't even all that bad to me....afterwards when I start shivering is worse, but the monotony...sitting there frustrating.

When the temperature of the water is around 45-50 or lower my hands after CT feel ...sort of a numb sensation. Is it possible I'm damaging my hands...is damage likely above freezing temperatures? I suppose the warmth goes away from the extremities...Is it perhaps partly a detox reaction in my hands. I'm wondering if I should worry about it or not. It lasts for days...don't like the feeling.

### caroline — 2013-02-20T09:51:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-adaption-time.5923/post-76637>

I don't know if I know enough to help you - but I can try. A lot of people wear caps and gloves and socks when doing C/T wool is the best. Eating carbs won't be helping - high fat is the way to go.

A lot of us take an ipad/ipod or some such into the tub with us and listen to podcasts or webinars or just music. I would go totally nuts without something to do or listen to!

Maybe reread the quick start guides again for C/T and the leptin reset - I think you are missing a couple of things or forgotten. I know I have to keep re reading the blogs.... as Dr. K. says - we get new eyes and understand things better the second and third time around. Sometimes I will swear I have never seen a blog before but then realise that yes I have - I just understand it better now.

I am beginning to realise that fruit is a big NO for me - I love fruit - but I am doing much, much better with none at all. My weight has stabilised much better and the cravings for fruit are much less.

Do you intend to keep eating a lot of carbs?

The BAB is - without question - the most important part of the reset. If you eat enough protein/ fat for your BAB - it will stop the cravings. We have all experienced this. I had to eat a huge amount of protein for breakfast but I quickly went to 2 meals a day and had absolutely no cravings what so ever.

I hope I have offered some help...

Also - maybe don't be so worried about a time frame - everyone is very, very different. Personal Context is everything!

### freesia — 2013-02-21T05:31:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-adaption-time.5923/post-76831>

I was radiating warmth _occasionally_ a few weeks in. But I still had periods where I seemed to go backwards for a bit - ie: baths were harder and recovery took longer.

If you have numbness of any kind I guess there's a chance you could be going at it too hard too fast? I had blue fingers in the beginning...even though I didn't put my hands in (and kept my feet and head insulated). I just slowed down and took it easier. Still inched my way forward. I didn't adapt in 2 weeks, I think because of my particular health challenges and need to pace.

I wasn't eating many carbs last winter when I adapted. I was doing leptin reset.
Winter diet, and winter stimulus to the CNS via skin work _together_. We're designed pretty well actually.

Hope that helps.

### ashryn — 2013-02-21T15:04:28+0200

At the same time make sure you are minding the artificial light. Once you have those things under control, other things will be easier. ### PrimalPam — 2013-02-23T22:54:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-adaption-time.5923/post-77333>

I was doing CT 5 days/week, 50F, 45 minutes, for 5 months and did not feel cold-adapted. I got busy over the holidays and have started back up, same schedule, and still do not feel cold-adapted. We are that broken; it is not a quick fix. We are a society of "This pill will work NOW"; well, I don't think one becomes cold-adapted for quite a while. I see tons of benefits from the cold (improved sleep, fat loss, and just pure enjoyment of it), but I do not consider myself cold-adapted.

I lay in the freezing cold tub sometimes thinking, well, I spent decades doing such damage to myself; it's going to take a lot more than a 45-minute bath to undo it.

Be patient, but stay away from the carbs too. Eat your BAB and try to only eat one other time a day, tons of seafood and fat. I do have a "mini-meal" for lunch, which is a large brazil nut, some 99% chocolate, and a few spoonfuls of coconut manna. This keeps any hunger at bay till dinner.

# Thread: It appears that one block of sleep is a new thing...article on the history of sleep
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/it-appears-that-one-block-of-sleep-is-a-new-thing-article-on-the-history-of-sleep.5924/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### lee — 2013-02-17T21:58:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/it-appears-that-one-block-of-sleep-is-a-new-thing-article-on-the-history-of-sleep.5924/post-76241>

The Myth of the Eight Hour Sleep
[http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/magazine-16964783](http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/magazine-16964783)
By Stephanie Hegarty

(It's a more pleasant read if you follow the link, but just in case the link dies, here's the article.)

We often worry about lying awake in the middle of the night - but it could be good for you. A growing body of evidence from both science and history suggests that the eight-hour sleep may be unnatural.

In the early 1990s, psychiatrist Thomas Wehr conducted an experiment in which a group of people were plunged into darkness for 14 hours every day for a month.

It took some time for their sleep to regulate but by the fourth week the subjects had settled into a very distinct sleeping pattern. They slept first for four hours, then woke for one or two hours before falling into a second four-hour sleep.

Though sleep scientists were impressed by the study, among the general public the idea that we must sleep for eight consecutive hours persists.
In 2001, historian Roger Ekirch of Virginia Tech published a seminal paper, drawn from 16 years of research, revealing a wealth of historical evidence that humans used to sleep in two distinct chunks.

His book At Day's Close: Night in Times Past, published four years later, unearths more than 500 references to a segmented sleeping pattern - in diaries, court records, medical books and literature, from Homer's Odyssey to an anthropological account of modern tribes in Nigeria.
Much like the experience of Wehr's subjects, these references describe a first sleep which began about two hours after dusk, followed by waking period of one or two hours and then a second sleep.

"It's not just the number of references - it is the way they refer to it, as if it was common knowledge," Ekirch says.

During this waking period people were quite active. They often got up, went to the toilet or smoked tobacco and some even visited neighbours. Most people stayed in bed, read, wrote and often prayed. Countless prayer manuals from the late 15th Century offered special prayers for the hours in between sleeps.

When segmented sleep was the norm
"He knew this, even in the horror with which he started from his first sleep, and threw up the window to dispel it by the presence of some object, beyond the room, which had not been, as it were, the witness of his dream." Charles Dickens, Barnaby Rudge (1840)
"Don Quixote followed nature, and being satisfied with his first sleep, did not solicit more. As for Sancho, he never wanted a second, for the first lasted him from night to morning." Miguel Cervantes, Don Quixote (1615)
"And at the wakening of your first sleepe You shall have a hott drinke made, And at the wakening of your next sleepe Your sorrowes will have a slake." Early English ballad, Old Robin of Portingale
The Tiv tribe in Nigeria employ the terms "first sleep" and "second sleep" to refer to specific periods of the night
Source: Roger Ekirch

And these hours weren't entirely solitary - people often chatted to bed-fellows or had sex.

A doctor's manual from 16th Century France even advised couples that the best time to conceive was not at the end of a long day's labour but "after the first sleep", when "they have more enjoyment" and "do it better".

Ekirch found that references to the first and second sleep started to disappear during the late 17th Century. This started among the urban upper classes in northern Europe and over the course of the next 200 years filtered down to the rest of Western society.

By the 1920s the idea of a first and second sleep had receded entirely from our social consciousness.

He attributes the initial shift to improvements in street lighting, domestic lighting and a surge in coffee houses - which were sometimes open all night. As the night became a place for legitimate activity and as that activity increased, the length of time people could dedicate to rest dwindled.
In his new book, Evening's Empire, historian Craig Koslofsky puts forward an account of how this happened.

"Associations with night before the 17th Century were not good," he says. The night was a place populated by people of disrepute - criminals, prostitutes and drunks.

"Even the wealthy, who could afford candlelight, had better things to spend their money on. There was no prestige or social value associated with staying up all night."

Roger Ekirch says this 1595 engraving by Jan Saenredam is evidence of activity at night

That changed in the wake of the Reformation and the counter-Reformation. Protestants and Catholics became accustomed to holding secret services at night, during periods of persecution. If earlier the night had belonged to reprobates, now respectable people became accustomed to exploiting the hours of darkness.

This trend migrated to the social sphere too, but only for those who could afford to live by candlelight. With the advent of street lighting, however, socialising at night began to filter down through the classes.

In 1667, Paris became the first city in the world to light its streets, using wax candles in glass lamps. It was followed by Lille in the same year and Amsterdam two years later, where a much more efficient oil-powered lamp was developed.

London didn't join their ranks until 1684 but by the end of the century, more than 50 of Europe's major towns and cities were lit at night.
Night became fashionable and spending hours lying in bed was considered a waste of time.

"People were becoming increasingly time-conscious and sensitive to efficiency, certainly before the 19th Century," says Roger Ekirch. "But the industrial revolution intensified that attitude by leaps and bounds."

Strong evidence of this shifting attitude is contained in a medical journal from 1829 which urged parents to force their children out of a pattern of first and second sleep.

A small city like Leipzig in central Germany employed 100 men to tend to 700 lamps

"If no disease or accident there intervene, they will need no further repose than that obtained in their first sleep, which custom will have caused to terminate by itself just at the usual hour.

"And then, if they turn upon their ear to take a second nap, they will be taught to look upon it as an intemperance not at all redounding to their credit."

Today, most people seem to have adapted quite well to the eight-hour sleep, but Ekirch believes many sleeping problems may have roots in the human body's natural preference for segmented sleep as well as the ubiquity of artificial light.

This could be the root of a condition called sleep maintenance insomnia, where people wake during the night and have trouble getting back to sleep, he suggests.

The condition first appears in literature at the end of the 19th Century, at the same time as accounts of segmented sleep disappear.
"For most of evolution we slept a certain way," says sleep psychologist Gregg Jacobs. "Waking up during the night is part of normal human physiology."

The idea that we must sleep in a consolidated block could be damaging, he says, if it makes people who wake up at night anxious, as this anxiety can itself prohibit sleeps and is likely to seep into waking life too.

Stages of sleep
Every 60-100 minutes we go through a cycle of four stages of sleep
Stage 1 is a drowsy, relaxed state between being awake and sleeping - breathing slows, muscles relax, heart rate drops
Stage 2 is slightly deeper sleep - you may feel awake and this means that, on many nights, you may be asleep and not know it
Stage 3 and Stage 4, or Deep Sleep - it is very hard to wake up from Deep Sleep because this is when there is the lowest amount of activity in your body
After Deep Sleep, we go back to Stage 2 for a few minutes, and then enter Dream Sleep - also called REM (rapid eye movement) sleep - which, as its name suggests, is when you dream

In a full sleep cycle, a person goes through all the stages of sleep from one to four, then back down through stages three and two, before entering dream sleep

Source: Gregg Jacobs

Russell Foster, a professor of circadian \[body clock\] neuroscience at Oxford, shares this point of view.

"Many people wake up at night and panic," he says. "I tell them that what they are experiencing is a throwback to the bi-modal sleep pattern."
But the majority of doctors still fail to acknowledge that a consolidated eight-hour sleep may be unnatural.

"Over 30% of the medical problems that doctors are faced with stem directly or indirectly from sleep. But sleep has been ignored in medical training and there are very few centres where sleep is studied," he says.

Jacobs suggests that the waking period between sleeps, when people were forced into periods of rest and relaxation, could have played an important part in the human capacity to regulate stress naturally.

In many historic accounts, Ekirch found that people used the time to meditate on their dreams.

"Today we spend less time doing those things," says Dr Jacobs. "It's not a coincidence that, in modern life, the number of people who report anxiety, stress, depression, alcoholism and drug abuse has gone up."

So the next time you wake up in the middle of the night, think of your pre-industrial ancestors and relax. Lying awake could be good for you.
Craig Koslofsky and Russell Foster appeared on The Forum from theBBC World Service. Listen to the programme here.

### fitness@home — 2013-02-17T23:56:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/it-appears-that-one-block-of-sleep-is-a-new-thing-article-on-the-history-of-sleep.5924/post-76260>

Thanks for posting...it was interesting read. I usually wake up enough to be _aware_ of it about 4.5-5.5 hours in. Most the time it is easy to fall back asleep.

Occasionally my body goes to wide awake stage (not going to shut back down). That's when I get up and just go sit outside on the patio in the moonlight. Figure, why fight it? The next night I sleep extremely well.

I have had some successes so far with all I am doing...better sleep/energy, lost 9 lbs, feel better mentally, i.e. more joy, contentment, really loving the cold and dark (which until this winter, my first with Epipaleo, CT etc) so I am happy....but still hoping for that miracle too.

### fitness@home — 2013-02-21T02:51:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leftover-duck-fat.5930/post-76802>

I had saved the duck carcass and this morning tossed it in the crockpot with some leeks, a few carrots and celery stalks to flavor the broth. There was just enough meat left on it to add to the soup too. It turned out wonderfully. This is what I'll be having for lunch for the next two days.

Never throw out chicken, turkey, or duck carcasses. Freeze them for making soup later on!

> Never throw out chicken, turkey, or duck carcasses. Freeze them for making soup later on!

I do this all the time and love the broth. I really like turkey broth but try to limit it because it's foul. Haven't had it for 4 months. But I've had a hankering for duck and may have to find one and make broth with that. Thanks for the idea.

# Thread: Finally got sick, and CTed.. yay!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-got-sick-and-cted-yay.5931/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Inger — 2013-02-18T11:28:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-got-sick-and-cted-yay.5931/post-76302>

I got the flu!!!
I wanted it so bad all winter because I wanted to test how it feels to CT with fever.. but I never got sick. Last week I did! I started to get runny nose.. and then because we had guests and I was busy and then I left one day without going into the ice hole.. next day I woke up sick. ha.
First day I had lowish fewer, next day I had high fever, and today I feel almost perfectly fine again, no fever and only a tiny bit coughing. I did CT both days, not long but several short dips. I can tell also with high fever it felt only nice to dip in the river.. wow! I kind of craved the cold, and the warm temps in my parents home downstairs really made me feel worse.. I spent time there this weekend because we had birthday party for dad. But I cannot tell how relieved I felt when I went upstairs to my own cool 15 degree C cave and even much colder bedroom!
Weird is I felt light in my body even if I was sick... it was not bad at all! Now I know, CT is great medicine for flu.

I guess having too many candles on made it hard for my lungs. I really feel they start to suffer. From regular lamp oil too. I do not like the smell either.. I like to use the olive oil lamps much better! And not too many of them. When alone I use only 1 or 2 lamps/candles and that is fine! When too many I can tell by my lungs they do not like it... I am not bothered by the darkness at all, I like it! But when others around.... it seems to bother them big time and they need plenty of candles to feel comfortable.

### fitness@home — 2013-02-18T12:41:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-got-sick-and-cted-yay.5931/post-76305>

You sound so excited to have had the flu! Glad you're feeling better Inger. At least you finally got to test CTs usefulness in fighting the flu. A cool room always feels better to me too.

I haven't had a cold or flu in so long (which is a good thing!). Stopped getting flu shots a couple years ago. Figure my immune system must be working pretty well. Have been exposed to it at work and through family members.

I'm like you and enjoy minimal lighting at night. Just one candle for use while making my way through the house is good. It actually must have rubbed off on DH. I've noted that he doesn't turn on any lights after I go to bed...he stays up at least three hours later than me.

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-18T16:49:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-got-sick-and-cted-yay.5931/post-76317>

Do you use beeswax or some kind of modern candle, like soy? I'm wondering if it's the smoke itself or the chemicals from the wax that is bothering you.

### Hope — 2013-02-18T18:23:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-got-sick-and-cted-yay.5931/post-76326>

Hi Inger,

that is truly awesome! And I am with you not the temps.....whew! And I have the same thing with candles...I don't like the smell when I sniff them out....

Thanks for sharing and all the best to you!

### BJK77 — 2013-02-18T18:38:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-got-sick-and-cted-yay.5931/post-76327>

# Thread: Day 3:  broke out in hives
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/day-3-broke-out-in-hives.5932/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BeFearless — 2013-02-18T18:39:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/day-3-broke-out-in-hives.5932/post-76329>

Anyone else break out in hives when starting this lifestyle?

I dove right in and went cold turkey on carbs. Put coconut oil in my coffee. Ate a variety of proteins -- eggs, fish, shrimp, beef, bacon, and some salad greens. On the morning of day 3, just after breakfast, I discovered hives all over my chest, back, neck, arms, and hands. Thankfully, they went away with Benadryl and hydrocortisone cream, so obviously it was some sort of allergic reaction to something I ate.

I suspect I may have overdone it with eggs. In the past I wouldn't ordinarily chow down 4 deviled eggs in a sitting. Maybe it was the coconut oil?

I know Dr. Kruse is big on testing, but I'm not insured and there is no room in my budget for allergy testing right now. I have environmental allergies year round (I suspect pet dander and pollen) and take 1 Zyrtec daily. To do allergy testing, I'd have to stop allergy meds for 2 full weeks. I can't think of any time of the year when I could tough it out for 2 full weeks -- I'd never sleep.

# Thread: Dr Shoemaker asked this question on leptin:
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-shoemaker-asked-this-question-on-leptin.5934/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Martin — 2013-02-18T22:07:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-shoemaker-asked-this-question-on-leptin.5934/post-76353>

**"**Daily Anaerobic Threshold Exercises

Patients who like to exercise have long used daily anaerobic threshold exercises to improve the chronic fatigue that comes from biotoxin illnesses. The Chronic Fatigue community often will cite the observed â€œpush, crashâ€￾ phenomenon as evidence for that syndrome but that idea is incorrect. Whenever there is capillary hypoperfusion, and that occurs with uncorrected elevation of inflammatory compounds like C4a, MMP9 and TGF beta-1, delivery of oxygen to capillary beds is reduced, mitochondrial production of ATP is lowered and glycogen reserves are exhausted. â€œPushâ€￾ means demanding that energy be consumed; capillary hypoperfusion means the energy will be consumed inefficiently. â€œCrashâ€￾ occurs when the glycogen reserves are depleted and patients start to burn protein for fuel (Alanine and glutamine are amino acids that are quickly converted to sugar) but that conversion isnâ€™t enough to supply the energy needs of the body. The crash usually lasts more than one day as it takes that long to replenish glycogen reserves.

**I have often wondered what the role of leptin was in this scenario, as leptin resistance, usually seen in MSH deficiency, protects fat reserves, prevents beta oxidation of fatty acids and prevents release of fatty acids from fat cells.** There may be more to the physiology. Leptin also acts to reduce the amount of adiponectin made and also reduces the amounts of the receptor for adiponectin. Adiponectin helps muscles (especially) burn fat directly.

The attached literature search may have some interest as long term anaerobic threshold exercise induces production of activity of the gene for adiponectin and the adiponectin receptor. We know that microRNA can suppress survival of mRNA so it is possible that a microRNA is made in biotoxin illnesses that suppresses both adiponectin and its receptor. We also know that antagomirs block microRNA and if there is a microRNA that we donâ€™t want to see blocking production of fat burners, we could theoretically block the blocker by a medication that would be the antagomir.

Such a compound would theoretically promote weight loss in those with leptin resistance, a phenomenon that occurs in at least 33% of our overweight population.**"**
(end quote)

Last time I saw Dr Shoemaker, he was overweight, and has recently retired due to health issues. He's even written a weight loss book, "Lose the Weight You Hate."

Any ideas as to what he's missing?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-19T00:56:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-shoemaker-asked-this-question-on-leptin.5934/post-76377>

EMF 7 will lay it out clearly........I promise.

# Thread: I still have my appendix and this is why it is a good thing to have.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-still-have-my-appendix-and-this-is-why-it-is-a-good-thing-to-have.5938/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glamazon — 2013-02-19T05:44:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-still-have-my-appendix-and-this-is-why-it-is-a-good-thing-to-have.5938/post-76418>

[Appendix good.](http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2013/02/14/appendix-evolved-over-30-times_n_2685499.html?utm_hp_ref=fb&src=sp&comm_ref=false)

In 2007, Parker and his colleagues suggested that the appendix has an immunological role, acting as a "safe house" for beneficial gut bacteria. These bacteria help train the immune system and can prevent diseases by out competing dangerous pathogenic bacteriaâ€”but there are times when the dangerous microbes gain the upper hand and overrun the gut. The researchers reasoned that when this happens, the beneficial bacteria could retreat to the safety of the appendix, which remains unaffected. Once the immune system has beaten the infection, the beneficial bacteria emerge from the appendix to quickly recolonize the gut.

# Thread: Amazing survival?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/amazing-survival.5944/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Inger — 2013-02-19T09:38:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/amazing-survival.5944/post-76431>

I just reread this blog
[http://http://www.jackkruse.com/do-food-electrons-impart-a-quantum-effect/](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/do-food-electrons-impart-a-quantum-effect/)

and found this quote.. and I got quite shocked..

> A human can live without food for 30 days. They can survive for without water for seven days. But the human brain cannot do without oxygen for 4 minutes or it dies.

Hubby had an accident about 11 years ago where he got shot and the bullet went through he's heart, the aorta, one lung.. he lost so much blood and the surgeon that operated on him for many hours told us later he's brain was 7 minutes without blood (??) and it was a amazing wonder he survived. They checked he's brain after, but all was good and no damage! Now I know, it was a real wonder! Wow!!!
It was in winter, and it was cold.. and it was 7 PM and dark.. it happened outside in the street. I wonder if all this had anything to say?

He even run about 20 meters after he got shot, he told me he wanted to run to a street lamp to check out where the bullet hit him.. The surgeon said after such a shot you fall down immidiately and do not run. The surgeon also told me the shock people get from such a experience helps the survival, and you can measure if this shock happened, he explained how but I do not remember.. but he told me the weird thing is, hubby didn't even had this shock reaction..?

It was an amazing suregon, a amazing personality for sure, a man with a huge heart.

I wonder if hubs is an animal..lol He sure survives the weirdest things.

I do remember how I visited him every single day and I told him to stay (he was in coma 2 weeks in between life and death connected to a breathing machine and other weird stuff) and not die and I crawled in he's bed and that was certainly not allowed as it was in the intensive care unit and I had to use hospital clothes and shoes all but the doctor never said a thing..lol Maybe I gave hubs electrons and the doctor knew that and that is why he let me do..

### caroline — 2013-02-19T09:42:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/amazing-survival.5944/post-76432>

That is an amazing story Inger ......... I think there are a lot of things that humans can't explain .....that defy all the odds.....

### HoneyChild — 2013-02-19T12:11:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/amazing-survival.5944/post-76442>

I don't think I can post links on this forum but do a search for "Surgeons use cold to suspend life" and you'll find a link to the BBC by Dr Kevin Fong. I watched his documentary when it was on TV a few years ago. I really wish I could watch it again as it was all about the effects of cold on the body. I'm pretty sure Wim Hoff was the guy featured on it too.

"Some 11 years ago, Anna Bagenholme, a 29-year-old skier, fell through ice covering a ravine in Norway.

Her heart stopped for more than three hours and her body temperature dropped to 13.7C (56.7F).

This is the longest and coldest cardiac arrest on record and that she survived at all is nothing short of miraculous."

# Thread: Extremes: Life, Death and the Limits of the Human Body by Dr Kevin Fong
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/extremes-life-death-and-the-limits-of-the-human-body-by-dr-kevin-fong.5948/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### HoneyChild — 2013-02-19T17:35:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/extremes-life-death-and-the-limits-of-the-human-body-by-dr-kevin-fong.5948/post-76480>

On another thread I mentioned watching a documentary by Dr Kevin Fong about the effects of cold on the body. Well, I just found out he's written a book called Extremes: Life, Death and the Limits of the Human Body which will be released next month

Copied from Amazon

In anaesthetist Dr Kevin Fong's television programmes he has often demonstrated the impact of extremes on the human body by using his own body as a 'guinea pig'. So Dr Fong is well placed to share his experience of the sheer audacity of medical practice at extreme physiological limits, where human life is balanced on a knife edge.

Through gripping accounts of extraordinary events and pioneering medicine, Dr Fong explores how our body responds when tested by the extremes of heat and cold, vacuum and altitude, age and disease. He shows how science, technology and medicine have taken what was once lethal in the world and made it survivable.

This is not only a book about medicine, but also about exploration in its broadest sense - and about how, by probing the very limits of our biology, we may ultimately return with a better appreciation of how our bodies work, of what life is, and what it means to be human.

**Review**
'It would be hard to find anyone better qualified to write a book on the limits of human physiology than Dr Kevin Fong. His experiences in human spaceflight at NASA, in frontline medicine, and his deep scientific knowledge, shine through. If you want to know what the human body can take, and why we must continue to push ourselves beyond the limit in the name of exploration, then read this book.' (Professor Brian Cox

**About the Author**
Dr Kevin Fong is a doctor of medicine with a special interest in human space exploration and extreme environment physiology. He holds degrees in medicine, astrophysics and engineering, and is an honorary senior lecturer in physiology at University College London. He has completed specialist training in anaesthesia and intensive care medicine, has worked with NASA's Human Adaptation and Countermeasures Office at Johnson Space Center in Houston and the medical operations group at Kennedy Space Center in Cape Canaveral. He currently works as a consultant anaesthetist at University College London Hospital, is founder and associate director of the Centre for Altitude, Space and Extreme Environment Medicine. He is a Wellcome Trust Public Engagement Fellow.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-19T19:01:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/extremes-life-death-and-the-limits-of-the-human-body-by-dr-kevin-fong.5948/post-76485>

He would love EMF 7 then........

### freesia — 2013-02-19T19:11:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/extremes-life-death-and-the-limits-of-the-human-body-by-dr-kevin-fong.5948/post-76490>

Looks like it could be a good read HoneyChild.
He's got enough interesting credentials to have some valuable insights!
Thanks for the head up.

# Thread: Coming to terms with dying - other people that is
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 74
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Aussie Nana — 2013-02-20T01:10:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76578>

It has hit home quite hard to me this last week that if I am to have better health than most of my peers then I had better get used to seeing friends and family go downhill and die. My sister has a heart condition. She looks terrible and thinks she looks good for someone her age. She had previously renamed all her heart symptoms as other things such as asthma, stress, tiredness, etc and continues to do so to a great extent. Basically she is very ill by my standards although her doctors are happy with the way she has bounced back after 10 days in the intensive coronary care unit.

So if we, as a community here, are going to be healthy and most of the rest of the world aren't then we need to develop a strong sense of acceptance when others choose other pathways to deal with their health issues. My sister is very pleased for me, but she doesn't want to hear what she could do that might make a difference. She is choosing differently to what I would like her to do. She thinks I'm a bore about health. So having said my piece all I can do is to respect her choices and continue loving her as she swallows her blood thinners and all the other 20 odd pills they have her on daily.

We also have to come to terms with people being very ill and dying while we stay healthy and know they could be healthier.

But its hard. I weep a little.

### fitness@home — 2013-02-20T01:44:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76585>

Aussie Nana,

Your words resonate with me. I have a very hard time accepting my parent's choices in healthcare. My Mom has many, many health issues and takes so many prescriptions.

Since I don't live in Michigan any longer I am lucky to see her twice per year. The rest of the family doesn't see the very noticeable decline in her health as I do. To me, it's almost shocking.

I have learned to accept their choices, even if I don't agree. What else does one do? I love my parents and want them around as long as possible.

### caroline — 2013-02-20T04:44:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76609>

This is going to be really tragic for all of us I fear. When I look back and think of all the things that may have helped my husband I shed many tears ....but I didn't know better then \[the ironic thing is he would have listened to me\]

I am now looking backwards with new eyes - doesn't stop the incredible sadness though and it is hard to forgive yourself.

On the other hand - my daughter needs these protocols desperately and won't listen to me ......yes , I too am a bore about health it seems.

Ultimately - we can only help ourself. In my case - the changes are very noticeable so why wouldn't that motivate someone you love so much?

Most seem to be stuck in such a CW rut and can't or don't want to put in the work. That is unimaginable to me but I can't change it.

JK always says ....."People don't care what you know until they know that you care" we will see.......

### Kiera2 — 2013-02-20T05:38:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76622>

Yes it's heartbreaking to watch people we love continue to risk their health. I share what we're doing every time someone comments on how well we look, but most don't want to know once they realise it's a lifestyle change, not just a temporary diet.

It's bad enough when it's the long distance in-laws, but my eldest son pretends to think that I'm a nutcase following some voodoo dr off the internet. He knows I'm not stupid, he just doesn't want to give up his lifestyle. He's been an online gamer for over a decade. It breaks my heart to know that he's giving up years of his life for it. And my eldest daughter has moved location with her employer; she's now deep in a bank's secure bunker/call centre. I can't image the EMF in there.

### caroline — 2013-02-20T08:38:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76634>

You are so right Coriander - they may listen if it is a temporary diet ..... but a llifestlye change .... they don't even pretend to be slightly interested. I don't get it ... this journey has been incredibly exciting and positive in so many ways and what did it cost us? some ice and a grocery budget spent on seafood instead of potato chips . We get to look at the sunrise and the sunset and the stars in the night sky. I love that I can't be bothered with TV. I love that it is more important to interact with endlessley interesting people whether it is family or friends or strangers. I love how I feel and I love the joy in my heart.

### fitness@home — 2013-02-20T12:46:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76642>

> [Coriander said:](/goto/post?id=76622)
>
> It's bad enough when it's the long distance in-laws, but my eldest son pretends to think that I'm a nutcase following some voodoo dr off the internet. He knows I'm not stupid, he just doesn't want to give up his lifestyle. He's been an online gamer for over a decade. It breaks my heart to know that he's giving up years of his life for it..

This! My stepson is the same way. He actually stood behind me for a few minutes while I was watching the Sleep webinar. JK was talking about EMFs and the kid rolls his eyes and actually says, "what rock is he hiding under? Today's world is advanced and uses devices and wifi all the time". He missed the entire damn point... Yes, we have filled our world with these things and are now realizing they are BAD for us as a species.

Seriously, he is 25 years old and has horrible eating habits. Poor diet = poor dental health = poor health for him in general. I fear that by age 35 he will have serious health issues. It was so ironic that it was the Sleep webinar that I was watching because his sleep pattern is so disrupted. There are times he is up all night.

Sorry for the vent...felt good to get it off my chest

### Destiny — 2013-02-20T13:34:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76643>

I think it is a process with the grown up kids. I am always e-mailing them tidbits of information and talking about Dr.K's stuff whenever I see them.

They take some thing and use it and ignore other. I think the problem is that they are young, relatively healthy and feel immortal.

I will keep persisting.... what other choice do I have? To give up on them, NEVER!

### SeaJane — 2013-02-20T16:50:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76660>

I have been walking this path of how much to push Jack's stuff with my mom. She was diagnosed with small cell lung cancer a year ago. She opted for chemotherapy to reduce the metastatic tumor that was causing pain and invading her abdominal cavity. She has always been health oriented and avoids the general medical establishment and their takes on things. She read a couple of books such as Anti-Cancer and another by a Dr. Block that advocate non-ketogenic diets, and opted for that approach even though I sent her info on the benefits of ketogenic diets with cancer. The chemo was successful in reducing the tumors and my mom kept pretty much on her "new" diet and started to add back in some dairy to help gain some weight back. Within 2 months the tumor had regrown and she opted for radiation therapy. This was in December. I was heart-broken after learning what I have from Jack's site. I just felt that the doctor's were killing her with their treatments. Radiation has been much harder for her and currently although she is technically done with treatments, her body is still suffering the cumulative action that is to take up to 4 weeks before it gets better. She has no appetite. She has now down to about 90lbs from her normal weight of 115lbs. Her energy (electrons) have been sucked out of her by the treatment. When I visited at the end of January, I suggested eating more oysters as she likes them and they are nutrient dense. This she did. As they live in St. Pete, I suggested she go and sit at the water's edge and put her feet in the water. This she wouldn't do because she already felt so cold--her body is struggling to generate enough energy to even keep her warm. There were things I said that I could see did not even register--her mind could not allow that to be so. What has become evident to me is that this illness and it's treatment has affected my mom's ability to stand back and question and to be open to new info. I am pretty sure her dopamine levels are in the tank and this is the underlying cause of suboptimal choices and her mind rationalizing shifts in her beliefs to support her behavior. It is challenging and painful to share with someone who has no space for something that may relieve suffering and bring health.

### Shijin13 — 2013-02-20T18:42:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76699>

This is challenging for me b/c in the past two years I've seen the impacts of bad diet, horrible sleep, and an unwillingness to change impact my mom. she turns 70 on 15 March. I've tried to engage her - and she's told me point blank that she's not changing a damn thing, she likes her life, and isn't going to "rock" the boat.
When DH and I had our talk on Saturday night after Jack dropped the MOAB on him, I told him I don't think my mom will make it to 80. I actually think her mom will outlive her. And that's scary. She's on statins, she hasn't had her BHRT adjusted since she started it 20yrs ago. In the past two years - she's suddenly looking far older than her age. I realize now that her in ability to get good sleep is what's doing this. On average I bet she's probably sleeping maybe 3-4hrs/night maybe 3-4 nights out of the week, and sleeping less the rest of the time. The scary thing, is I'm not sure my dad will survive her passing - he's not really on speaking terms with one of his brothers, and his other brother is a total flake. What's crazy is I'm coming to the realization, that while I may not get along well with my Mother-in-law, she's going to be the largest presence in my family - and she's making the same changes we are, she wants to be here with us and the kids, and it saddens me that the choices my mom and dad are making - are telling me they aren't. It makes me sad. It also hits home, that regardless of how MIL and I feel about each other, the two of us must bury the hatchet, b/c she's going to be here, where as my parents won't. I already feel that loss, even though they're not gone.

I'd love it if my mom and dad would willing take on changing their lifestyle like MIL is, if not for me then for Chaos and Havoc. As I look at the conversations I've had w/them over the past years - I realize now that they're already in the process of checking out... it makes me sad b/c I want to fight for them, but they won't fight for themselves, let alone me or the kids.

As much as it makes me sad, I've also realized that my tenacity and determination I have came from my parents, they're not going to change regardless of how much I try to educate them... so I have to let them go. They'll always be apart of my life but they're not present nor apart of where we're going.

### fitness@home — 2013-02-20T20:54:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76734>

Another well-written response Gretchen. Yes, time to bury the hatchet with your MIL. Who knows, you may gain a fulfilling relationship from it. At least she is open to change.

As I look at the conversations I've had w/them over the past years - I realize now that they're already in the process of checking out... it makes me sad b/c I want to fight for them, but they won't fight for themselves, let alone me or the kids.
>
> As much as it makes me sad, I've also realized that my tenacity and determination I have came from my parents, they're not going to change regardless of how much I try to educate them... so I have to let them go. They'll always be apart of my life but they're not present nor apart of where we're going.

For the sake of your own sanity, flip it around a bit - what if she were in a fatal accident and taken suddenly. I have had the unfortunate luck to have many people killed in my life and around me in sudden and tragic accidents. I think you know of this from your military experience, no? Once you realllllly get inside that life can just stop, and often does - it sure makes all those things you can't seem to change with other people pop into focus - as well as what you really want to be spending your time doing.

I'm someone that has always re-invented and because until recently I never had other people I cared to be around - picking up and moving was a major adventure and always a positive thing. If it's not, then why do it.

The other thing few will say is it really is OK to let go of someone without them actually getting hit by the bus. If they are not your tribe in any other way than name - you really can just walk away. Tribes can be made as well as born into.

Now, whar you going to? Did you hear, my little burb is the 2nd happiest place in the US!

[http://www.dailycamera.com/ci\_22623092/tweet-this-longmont-is-second-happiest-city?source=rss](http://www.dailycamera.com/ci_22623092/tweet-this-longmont-is-second-happiest-city?source=rss)

Come here! Several businesses that I bet are right up the employment ally (Major FAA center, digital globe, ibm, seagate) <---hehe I do see the irony of that.

You can have BOTH here, a connection to the world plus some nature. Plus, there would be an optimal playdate!

### KiwiLauren — 2013-02-21T04:36:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76820>

I wasn't commenting on what you know. Of course I don't know what you know. I don't pretend to (hence why I read your blog). Nor was I trying to comment about whether you are right or wrong about a particular issue. I am ready to hear what you have to say. I have a good bit of experience holding uncomfortable truths.

I was trying to make the point that no one can know everything. It is ridiculous to think one person can. The learning and growing is in where we are wrong. But as that video pointed out, being wrong feels like being right until you realise it... hence we all have blind spots.

I was also trying to say that each of us has our own knowing, our own path, our own way of making decisions about our life. I was responding to this entire thread, not just your statement. No one can see another's heart fully. And even when another's choices do not match what we believe to be right, that does not mean their choice is the wrong one for them. I do not endorse a hierarchy, with the smartest people being the healthiest or those who survive when others perish. There are just too many other variables. You cannot know what they know. And even when people make choices that seem poor ones, I believe they deserve our compassion, not our pity.

KiwiLauren, I think you missed Jack's point....
I 100% know how we die is up to us. My grandmom died at over 90 yo a few months ago right around Christmas this winter. My aunt was there when she died too and told my dad later about it. Grandma had alzheimer for many years already, but still she was always a very happy person. She died so peacefully, it was just beautiful. But she had a sun shining in her heart always and that made all the difference even if she could have been healthy if she had knewn but she didn't.

Once she said to my dad hugging him as he visited her in the hospital.. Oh Willy.. you are so beautiful!!! Lol. And she barely remebered he was her son.. but she loved him dearly still..
The state of her mind was beautiful. And how she died was too... She loved so much. So even if her body and "mind" were not working well her heart was stronger. This is why I know, love matters and that is what makes all the difference. Love always seek the truth too, and it is not afraid to do..

### SeaHorse — 2013-02-21T22:48:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76931>

I think you may be talking about the same thing at different levels...yes we are all tied together in life and death on this planet, so big events that have no origin of our individual making. i.e. earthquakes, can be seen to have an origin in our collective making i.e. messing up the planet which logically involves us as individuals.

How an individual may face death or dying certainly can be within their control....but not one of us is able to stop an earthquake, so in that regard the deaths in New Zealand were not under their control. My mother chose to stop eating in order to die so her control was direct and absolute, but she didn't know exactly when her heart would stop...

How we face the end of our lives is often chosen for us by our lifestyles decisions...look at all the people with degenerative disorders who can't think or function well at all. Their ability to think well is gone but was taken away by choices they made throughout their lives (unconsciously or consciously). I think KL is reminding us that there is no place for judgement in this discussion and talking about people "choosing" how they die when life is ripped from them in an earthquake isn't useful. Talking about interconnectedness is.

So we have control and no control and perhaps that's the paradox we live in.

When did this forum become a Seinfeld episode. Here is my comedic analysis of what is going to happen next time someone asks a specific question about the science:

1\. Jack will state for the gazillionth time that he knows what's what and the rest of the paleosphere will be sorry, and teases Blogs to come. Ends with random quote-bot nonsense.
2\. Inger turns into the woodland nymph and dances around basking in her love and light and tries to will the bliss across the world to us sad ignorant ones through quantum digereedoodle.
3\. Anna will try so hard to steer the ship to the actual question at hand and will piss off someone.
3\. Kiwi Lauren then humbles many with completely thoughtful, and actually relevant to the discussion (we don't deserve you KL!).
4\. I pop in with snark and fun because all the crazy can bring one down and laughter is good for my health.
5\. 3 more followers get offended and pout like it's the 90's and internet forums just came about (the forum equiv of emailing cat pictures to your aunt)

Anybody got anything to add?

### Aussie Nana — 2013-02-22T07:36:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76999>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=76922)
>
> maybe hard for you to accept but that is what the data says........it is also why your grandmother can tell when bad weather is coming. It blocks the Schumann.......bad behavior does the same thing.......it limits our spark of life........by generating scalar vectors that disrupt circadian signaling.

I never thought of knowing about the coming of rain before it starts as being due to a lack of something, Schumann or otherwise - all I know is that something changes and I have to get the washing in before the rain happens. I can be deep in a book, or cooking, or anything and all of a sudden I move - something has changed. I have to do things before the rain comes - washing in, other stuff outside put away or windows closed. But if that something I used to put down to change in pressure is the Schumann then I'm OK with that too.

My sister in intensive care found herself out of bed a good second before the old lady in the bed next to her died setting off the alarms. She had been asleep then felt a change - she put it down to a change in her breathing, but the good ex-nurse picked the change up before the alarms started. That was a change in life force. We can feel it. Sitting with a dead person is quite different from sitting by someone who is alive but asleep or in a coma.

But back to KLs point. I prefer to avoid the idea of equating individual responsibility to corporate responsibility. I think that sometimes s\_\_t happens. A mountaineer might choose to go up the mountains and that puts them at risk of being caught in an avalanche. I don't think they are personally responsible for choosing to die under the avalanche if they have taken reasonable care, any more than I don't think that the 8 month old baby chose to die at the hands of a drunk driver while asleep in her own cot in her room (the car ploughed through the wall), or that the people who died in Christchurch as a result of the earthquake.

I don't think the animals that lived under a volcano in prehuman times were responsible for the volcano exploding, nor now.

How far we can push human responsibilty is up for discussion - we are responsible for much more than we ever thought possible - that is now obvious, but volcanoes and earthquakes have been around longer than humans.

### Inger — 2013-02-22T08:07:34+0200

It is all about our thinking. That is why it feels all confusing.
I just want to ask.. where does such a thinking brings me, when I think I cannot choose how I die.. I cannot choose how my life will be... it is not up to me...
It takes away my responsibility, my consciousness, the fact that every little thing I do or think has consequenses. small or big. But it has.

So I choose to not think like that. To me it equals stagnation.. death...

I see it around me all the time. It is heartbreaking, and I mean it for real. I cannot express it strong enough but it is in our thinking that I am sure of. IDK why and how like Jack knows, I know only partly, but it is sad for sure.

(about volkanoes and earthquakes.. or tornadoes.. we know today in what places those usually happends.. and we can choose to not live in such an area, or not in a big city where such things get really dangerous.. or we can choose to, and then we know the risks. Think a bit further and you will realize there is a lot more... it just depends how conscious you are..)

Maybe we all need to expand our consciousness BIG time. But it demands hard work now with all those EMFs around.. ha. But impossible? No.

And.. Cavemam.. I like that you think about me as a woodland nympf..lol

Unfortunately, in the meantime, four of my friends (in their 40s & early 50s, with young children) have contracted cancer (one MS) and died, another just developed cancer (while pregnant, with 5 young children, she is not yet 40), my sister is 30 and has had 3 brushes with cancer so far, two other close relatives had died from preventable modern disease, my in-laws are living in physical misery (despite eating a moderate fat, low grain, no wheat life), & my parents are rapidly plummeting into poor health (ever since listening to the doc & starting statins & cutting out beef).

This is all in the six years since I first woke up, threw out processed food, dramatically increased fat, got rid of chemicals in my home and on my person, started getting outside more, more sun, more temp swings, and started cutting back on the EMF. It all began changing, dramatically, six years ago, and has changed more and more ever since, and I'm afraid that by the time they start asking me what might help them, they will all be dead.

### Dali Dula — 2013-02-25T17:08:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-77576>

> [MamaGrok said:](/goto/post?id=77574)
>
> Well, DD, that was exactly my point. I can't do anything but wait. And by the time they're ready, they're likely going to be dead.

We must not let our frustration turn to anger or impatience. I read a quote years ago from the Dalai Lama. When asked by an interviewer what the most important thing we can do he said, "Greet everyone with a smile." At the time I thought this was simplistic nonsense. Now that I have some understanding of oxytocin and it's mechanism I think he may be right. Bringing a smile to the table is a game changer. A smile creates an oxytocin response in both the smiler and the smiled upon. This creates receptivity. Preaching, warning and telling people what they should or shouldn't do creates resistance. Make yourself beautiful, smile onto it. That smile may save someone's life.

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-25T17:14:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-77579>

Nope, no anger or impatience with my loved ones. My long years throwing myself into Catholic meditation and study of the saints and deep prayer have taught me that well.

Several years ago, some ladies & I were in a small prayer/study group, and we made a resolution to greet everyone with a smile the coming week. I was so moved by the effect it has on humanity and myself that I've made it a habit ever since. It is powerful. The only times it gets really tough is when my energy levels are very, very low.

### Birdy — 2013-02-25T18:05:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-77589>

I guess I don't have too many problems with friends and family. I am known as pretty out there anyway. Due to my success on a low carb diet, many friends and family members have gone either straight low carb or have been adding more saturated fats to their diet. Of course, my immediate family eats the way I cook, but so far, other than CT, my family, near and far, has not been willing to try any of the other suggestions. I do have some close relatives with some serious illnesses, and they are following the low-fat near-vegetarian approach. It isn't working, but I don't say anything anymore to them because it is too disruptive. But it is hard to read their blogs, beating themselves up for going off plan at a bbq, and then exercising like mad to get back on track. And unfortunately, after initial weight loss changes blood lipids, things go back to the current levels, and they are pressured by their docs to do more. Currently I do not share Kruse's recommendations with my friends. I think they are looking for more weight loss before they jump in. My immediate family has started going to the beach with me, but so far no serious dunking. I am hoping that some family members will take up some interest in the EMF series, in fact, I think they would be perfect in an emf-remediation business. I am also hoping that some sanity will be introduced/restored on the Kruse-ship and I can recommend reading again. Lately, this site has been my guilty pleasure/train-wreck and most people surrounding me have neither the time of the patience to sift through this.

# Thread: My n-1 with a grounding sheet
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-n-1-with-a-grounding-sheet.5962/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2013-02-20T15:20:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-n-1-with-a-grounding-sheet.5962/post-76648>

I've been somewhat testing out the use of a grounding sheet the last few weeks and I'm a little concerned with what I'm finding. I know I read on here (\*I think) that someone mentioned the grounding mats and sheets may just spread out dirty electricity if you're plugging them into a grounded outlet, which is what I'm doing.

I have a half sheet on my bed and we've been turning the breakers off for the bedrooms at night, so of course there's no electricity through the outlets and the sheet should be naturally grounded, right? I definitely can't say I'm sleeping any better or see any improvements, BUT when DH turns the breaker back on in the AM and I stay in bed for a little longer I am noticing a very uncomfortable, almost painful tingle in my feet and lower legs that touch the sheet.

Has anyone else read that using a grounding mat/sheet via an outlet can actually be more harmful than good? I'm going to get DH to attach it to a grounding rod outside as soon as the ground thaws, but who knows when that will be.

### freshveggies — 2013-02-20T16:24:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-n-1-with-a-grounding-sheet.5962/post-76653>

> [BJK77 said:](/goto/post?id=76648)
>
> I've been somewhat testing out the use of a grounding sheet the last few weeks and I'm a little concerned with what I'm finding. I know I read on here (\*I think) that someone mentioned the grounding mats and sheets may just spread out dirty electricity if you're plugging them into a grounded outlet, which is what I'm doing.
>
> I have a half sheet on my bed and we've been turning the breakers off for the bedrooms at night, so of course there's no electricity through the outlets and the sheet should be naturally grounded, right? I definitely can't say I'm sleeping any better or see any improvements, BUT when DH turns the breaker back on in the AM and I stay in bed for a little longer I am noticing a very uncomfortable, almost painful tingle in my feet and lower legs that touch the sheet.
>
> Has anyone else read that using a grounding mat/sheet via an outlet can actually be more harmful than good? I'm going to get DH to attach it to a grounding rod outside as soon as the ground thaws, but who knows when that will be.

Dr. Klinghardt says to not plug into outlet. Need to ground outside.

### diane — 2013-02-20T16:42:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-n-1-with-a-grounding-sheet.5962/post-76655>

I can't ground outside - my entire complex sits over a parking garage, so no point, really. So I got an earthing mat - the cheapest alternative to see if it makes any difference. Got a strap as well. Used the strap at work yesterday - no tired feeling in the afternoon (which is pretty typical after a long weekend LOL), then used the mat when I got home. I ended up in bed at 8pm I was so tired! I slept on the mat, and I had a fantastic sleep. From one day, can't tell if it's the mat or not - but it didn't hurt.

For my own little test, I've been testing it with my dogs as they are way more in tune with the environment than I am. They have a huge bed that will fit both of them, but still gives space. I have the mat on the bed and they have 3 choices - lie on the bed not touching the mat, lie on the bed touching the mat, or lie somewhere else (which they often do).They aren't going out of their way to avoid the mat and one is lying on it now. I want to give them a choice, and see what they do.

### BJK77 — 2013-02-20T16:49:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-n-1-with-a-grounding-sheet.5962/post-76659>

> [freshveggies said:](/goto/post?id=76653)
>
> Dr. Klinghardt says to not plug into outlet. Need to ground outside.

That must be where I heard it. I've read/watched/listened to so much lately I'm having a hard time keeping track of it all.

> [diane said:](/goto/post?id=76655)
>
> I can't ground outside - my entire complex sits over a parking garage, so no point, really. So I got an earthing mat - the cheapest alternative to see if it makes any difference. Got a strap as well. Used the strap at work yesterday - no tired feeling in the afternoon (which is pretty typical after a long weekend LOL), then used the mat when I got home. I ended up in bed at 8pm I was so tired! I slept on the mat, and I had a fantastic sleep. From one day, can't tell if it's the mat or not - but it didn't hurt.
>
> For my own little test, I've been testing it with my dogs as they are way more in tune with the environment than I am. They have a huge bed that will fit both of them, but still gives space. I have the mat on the bed and they have 3 choices - lie on the bed not touching the mat, lie on the bed touching the mat, or lie somewhere else (which they often do).They aren't going out of their way to avoid the mat and one is lying on it now. I want to give them a choice, and see what they do.

What a great idea Diane. I will have to try this with my dogs as well . . . although I'm afraid one of them has completely lost his intuition thanks to our efforts to domesticate him

I'm fortunate that at home I can ground the sheet outside, but I have also been using a mat off and on in the office and I worry about DH being in a car 4-6 hours each day. It isn't always practical or possible for one to ground outside constantly and I'm hoping we can find a working solution.

### Chrysanthemama — 2013-02-22T06:02:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-7-is-now-live-quantum-prometheus.5975/post-76989>

All I can say is, "Wow!!" I love it all. I wish I could channel my physicist Grandfather, whom, I am certain, would have loved reading this post. I am so fortunate to have been exposed to quantum physics as a kid, and to live where I live. I grew up as a minority living amongst native pueblo people, one of only a handful of white people. Karma is definitely a bitch. I think about the native kids I knew who were designated as "troubled" or as having "behavioral" issues. Autism had not yet become a diagnosis. Nor had OCD, or "Oppositional Defiance Disorder" or any of the modern day diagnoses. Yet, many of the native kids I knew were fully oppositional. They hated school, authority, rules, and punishment. The ones who were most troublesome ended up being sent to military school!! It wasn't until I was in my first year of college that I realized what the white "settlers" had done. The level of heartbreak I experienced was overwhelming. I can only repeat what Hope said, "Karma is a bitch."

Jack, kudos! I am astonished, amazed, dumbfounded, and alert with piqued interest. I cannot wait to hear what else you are going to say. The grounding thing resonated immediately. In fact, the whole grounding and CT thing right now has become obsessive with me. I got my hubby hooked, too, and he is out there, in the cold, going to our new spring-fed CT spot several times a week, despite the 1.5 mile hike in. I leave, with an owl who-who-whoing at me, and I can't wait to go back.

Thank you. EMF7 is phenomenal. Nothing like a caldera, shit tons of basalt, ash flows, mountains, snow, ice, and springs to make you feel good, right?

I am pretty sure he left Los Alamos shortly after the war. The house where he lived, which is a historical site, claims he lived there from 1943 to 1945. He then went on to teach at Princeton. But, with all the federal funding shortfalls, security breaches, and general hullabaloo surrounding the Lab, I just keep waiting for the day when this town returns back to its small-town on a mountain roots. In the mean time, waiting to hear about the EMF Rx and I'll keep grounding because it feels so good.

### Psyche — 2015-08-25T20:44:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-7-is-now-live-quantum-prometheus.5975/post-175683>

This week, I went back to read Quantum Prometheus, and I'm taking my time with it.

I'm having a really, really hard time with the entire EMF thing. I have accepted the truth of it, and it's crushing. Absolutely crushing. It makes me feel hopeless, and sad for my 15 year old son and his future, and then there's the whole part where you feel so isolated because no one understands what the fuck you're talking about and if they actually understand what you're saying, they do not believe it. If my boyfriend doesn't believe flouride is bad for him, will there ever be hope on the emf front???

I know i have to take the perspective that it's my duty to learn and take care of myself and make sure I do whatever I can to fix ME, so that I can be there to fix "them" when/if they ever want/need it, but this information is earth shattering stuff. Knowing we are in an extinction event is a huge weight to bear.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-25T20:47:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-7-is-now-live-quantum-prometheus.5975/post-175684>

Its not crushing...........Its reality and there is a way out.

The full story of quantum prometheus is in Marino's book, Going Somewhere, read it. Then I want to leave you with A comment Becker wrote in the Body Electric.

"Medicine began as an art. The masterpiece—a cure—resulted from the patient's will combined with the physician's intuition and skill in using remedies culled from millennia of observant trial and error. In the last two centuries medicine more and more has come to be a science, or more accurately the application of one science, namely biochemistry. Medical techniques have come to be tested as much against current concepts in biochemistry as against their empirical results. Techniques that don't fit such chemical concepts—even if they seem to work—have been abandoned as pseudoscientific or downright fraudulent."
\- Robert O. Becker, MD

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-25T20:48:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-7-is-now-live-quantum-prometheus.5975/post-175685>

My take: How should the art of medicine merge with the science? Medicine is more art than science but today they want to think it is based upon evidence. It is a fallacy because if you ask the wrong questions in your research you get the wrong results and you believe them and the paradigm freezes it in its dogma. Do you look "at science" or do you look thru science through a lens? I submit that that most of us are prisoners to modern science perspectives because we see it via "their lenses". I look at nature and turn it 90 degrees, 180 degrees, 270 degrees, and degrees in between. Every time I do this, my perspective of a problem changes and I learn a little bit more about what I missed from the basic perspective from which I was taught. I learned to do this from DaVinci and Michelangelo who were masters of altering the ground and foreground in their works. They changed sizes and shapes of proportions on their art to make the visualization of the observer more life like. They were masters at altering the lens that we see the world through to make it more accurate based upon where we were in space and time I view science in the same way. I need to alter the perception of what we currently believe so you can see the parts of science that are unobservable in action. Just because you can't or don't see it does not make it immaterial, a fallacy, or pseudoscience. On the contrary, it turns our what you can't see and do not know are the most important parts of science. Do you look at that lens and observe how it might bend reality? You should, because you'll see the future is not as dark as you think. ----- Dr. Jack Kruse DMD, MD

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-25T21:03:01+0300

Keep intensely focused on the outcome, not the obstacles. When you become coherent with the music within you, that is when you can make sense of the distraction going on around you. Simplicity is ultimately a matter of focus. Once you realize this........your fear becomes your strength.

### Psyche — 2015-08-25T21:03:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-7-is-now-live-quantum-prometheus.5975/post-175693>

Thank you, Dr. Kruse. I am having this reaction because I feel like the situation IS hopeless, and I will take you on your word that it is not. Coincidentally, I ordered Going Somewhere a few days ago after reading the introduction and first chapter of it as a Kindle preview. Marino's website is also very good- especially the blog posts. His very first post is one I especially like:

Concept Not Precept

 [](http://andrewamarino.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/2010/09/concept-precept.png)“Hard scientific evidence.” I’ve heard that phrase throughout my career, and I guess I’ve used it myself many times. In the beginning I had just naturally regarded “hard scientific evidence” as if it were more or less a definite thing. There was the Queen Mary, Central Park, the Mona Lisa, and “hard scientific evidence.” It had never occurred to me that recognizing “hard scientific evidence” would be any more difficult than recognizing the ship, the park, or the painting. Only much later did I realize that “hard scientific evidence” was a concept, not a precept. After that realization, the debate about health risks from environmental EMFs made much more sense.

Sooner or later, I think the principle will be accepted that knowledge regarding the link between EMFs and disease is imperfect, now and forever. Really it’s only common sense. The consequences of everything we do, or that is done to us, are imperfectly predictable, and the consequences of EMFs are no exception. “Hard scientific evidence” is not in the world, it is in the mind.

# Thread: Earth Runners- minimalist earthing sandals [Feedback]
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earth-runners-minimalist-earthing-sandals-feedback.5990/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread

### EarthRunner1987 — 2013-02-22T09:00:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earth-runners-minimalist-earthing-sandals-feedback.5990/post-77005>

After I read the Earthing book it seemed to describe my experiences while spending so much time barefoot out in nature. Ever since I transitioned to a more grounded lifestyle I have experienced remarkable results beyond belief. The timing of all this aligned so well with my graduation from college and passion to start a meaningful career. It was at this point a year ago I decided to combine all my skills and started a minimalist earthing sandal company called Earth Runners. I aimed to make conductive sandal that can be worn anywhere. Please check them out and let me know what you think.
[www.earthrunners.com](http://www.earthrunners.com)

I am constantly working to improve our design, please share any feedback you may have.

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" } [](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/both-models2-jpg.41/)
Cheers,
Mike

### Kiera2 — 2013-02-22T09:23:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earth-runners-minimalist-earthing-sandals-feedback.5990/post-77007>

Oh Mike, you beat me! My Earth Runners arrived this afternoon, and I put them on immediately.

I've tried a few different ways to lace them, and they are so incredibly comfortable! My dh has just put his on, and he says they feel like he's really barefoot. He has only ever worn 'normal' shoes, so he feels the lack of a heel, but I've worn minimalist barefoot shoes for years so the only challenge for me is the leather lace. I didn't think I would llke the feel of the long lace wound around my ankle, but it's fine; doesn't feel at all like I'm choking my foot, though I thought it would.

So, I highly recommend Mike's Earth Runners! My experience with him has been fantastic; great customer service. I ordered the sandals on the 12th, they were in the mail on the 16th, and on my feet today. I have received a very high quality product, and now I'm off for a walk!

### nonchalant — 2013-02-22T17:05:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earth-runners-minimalist-earthing-sandals-feedback.5990/post-77031>

I like the straps near the ankles. Much better than a flip-flop design. The foot can act naturally while walking.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earth-runners-minimalist-earthing-sandals-feedback.5990/post-77049>
