# Thread: Is the scale the right measurement for success
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-scale-the-right-measurement-for-success.6164/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### diane — 2013-03-04T17:04:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-scale-the-right-measurement-for-success.6164/post-78915>

I think that most people evaluate the success of what they're doing mainly by the numbers on the scale. If they lose weight, it's successful, if they don't it's not. And people then watching others will evaluate by asking "how much weight did you lose".

I'm not saying that losing body fat isn't important for health, but we here know that having body fat isn't the only sign that people are sick.

I've kinda given up looking at the scale, yet I think it can play a role in giving feedback and showing overall progress. I just don't think that it's the only measurement people should rely on, which is what our society seems to do now.

I also wonder when people here report that they haven't lost weight, does that give new people the impression that all of this doesn't work? I'm not criticizing as I know that people are reporting - my comment is more around the external factor of using weight as THE measure of success.

What is/are the right ones? Does it change by person? Is it labs, or how you feel? Is it the ability to meet your life's goals with energy and passion? What is the role of the scale in measuring success?

I've clearly been thinking about this a while lol.

### Shijin13 — 2013-03-04T17:16:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-scale-the-right-measurement-for-success.6164/post-78916>

The only time I step on a scale these days is when I go into the dr's for a discussion on my labs. that's it. I've stopped worrying about my weight. for me the best indicator is how my "skinny" clothes fit. suddenly I'm back into all my pre-pregnancy business clothes. I call that a win... I no longer care about weight b/c its not a true measurement of health. yes I want my belly fat to go away - and it will but not until I get everything else right first.
I'm seeing changes. I think its going to be individual. but that's me.

### BJK77 — 2013-03-04T17:24:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-scale-the-right-measurement-for-success.6164/post-78918>

I think this is just one more area where we, as a society, are completely misguided. I am not over or underweight and most would probably assume I'm perfectly healthy. I know this to be completely untrue. In fact, I would go so far as to say there are many overweight people who are probably healthier than I am based on labs, energy levels, etc.

I'd love to say I never focus on my weight, but if I feel my clothes getting a bit tight I still have that shallow side of me that gets upset. I'd like to say it's because I'm concerned about my health, and that is partly true, but there's also that whole vanity thing too! I can, however, honestly say that I very rarely know my weight. From day one, I refused to have a scale in my house and still don't. The only time I'm ever weighed is in a doctor's office and these days that is a very rare occurrence.

### jenaf — 2013-03-04T19:02:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-scale-the-right-measurement-for-success.6164/post-78925>

I definitely think the scale is used too much as a measurement of success, its a tool, to basically measure symptoms... same as journaling how many hours of sleep you get per night, how many times you wake up etc. I think the labs should be the gold standard for measurement of success. They will tell you if your body is becoming healthy again. Last year, eating paleo I lost 3 dress sizes, and gained muscle training for a hard core kettlebell certification but I didn't loose any weight on the scale. I also ruined my health so much (already being in adrenal fatigue and not realizing it) the doctors thought i had a hole in my heart, or some weird metabolic condition. Now being fairly strict epi-paleo, doing the leptin rx, sleep rx, mitochondrial rx, swimming in the ocean 2-3x week, starting to drink lots of spring water and cutting down my EMF exposure I have not lost any weight on the scale, but my sleep is much much better, and so are my energy levels.

I too dont have a scale at home

### DarleenMB — 2013-03-04T20:25:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-scale-the-right-measurement-for-success.6164/post-78930>

The scale is a lousy measure of success but if you've been addicted to it for decades, as I have, it's very very difficult to let go of it. I've written before about how hard it is for me to not slip back into old ways of thinking and doing (eat less and you'll lose weight; weigh every day because you have to watch that scale; I hate myself because I'm fat which really means "you're a lazy big fat slob" which I know isn't true but emotionally -- forget it).

It's hard when you are broken, and the only outward measure is your big fat ass, to recognize that something else needs fixing and that sooner or later (if you stay the course) it will correct ITSELF without any interference, re: help, from you. Fixing what's broken is akin to what Jack has been talking about in the EMF blogs. You cannot SEE it (unless you constantly test test test which most of us cannot afford to do) so it must be the CW at work. i.e. you eat too much and are a lazy assed slug.

This is my struggle with the scale. Intellectually I know it's horse shit. Emotionally it's a whole 'nother ballgame.

### nonchalant — 2013-03-04T22:33:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-scale-the-right-measurement-for-success.6164/post-78959>

I recently pulled out the scale to document my weight, as a benchmark. Then I realized it was a body fat scale, and it sends a small voltage through my body. This is probably not a Kruse-recommended type of scale. I need to bring my old spring scale back from long-term storage (garage).

### DarleenMB — 2013-03-04T23:21:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-scale-the-right-measurement-for-success.6164/post-78971>

I have a similar type and it only does it when you step on the surface with damp feet so I'm not gonna worry about it.

### Rachelle — 2013-03-04T23:26:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-scale-the-right-measurement-for-success.6164/post-78974>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=78916)
>
> The only time I step on a scale these days is when I go into the dr's for a discussion on my labs. that's it. I've stopped worrying about my weight. for me the best indicator is how my "skinny" clothes fit. suddenly I'm back into all my pre-pregnancy business clothes. I call that a win... I no longer care about weight b/c its not a true measurement of health. yes I want my belly fat to go away - and it will but not until I get everything else right first.
> I'm seeing changes. I think its going to be individual. but that's me.

If I waited for everything to get right first, I would have never lost my belly fat. And, I can say there is still much wrong with me but I am working on it the best way I can.

### Patty Cakes — 2013-03-05T00:10:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-scale-the-right-measurement-for-success.6164/post-78978>

I think the scale is one measurement but not one everyone needs to use. We all can decide if it is appropriate for our context. I was crazy addicted to it in the beginning but now have relaxed that my energy has come back and I feel better. I use it now only when I feel something is going on and I need to document a weight change. I use it as my measurement for estrogen being stored in my body in a negative way. But that is for my context and only through my own trial and error have I discovered that works. But there could be other things going on as well.

### diane — 2013-03-05T00:30:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-scale-the-right-measurement-for-success.6164/post-78985>

Patty - that's what I was thinking in terms of how it could be useful to note changes and give feedback, rather than a "pass or fail"measurement. I would think that increases in the scale can also show dehydration/water retention as well.

# Thread: High sex drive in women....I can't be alone
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-sex-drive-in-women-i-cant-be-alone.6165/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Reset 2013
**Posts:** 27

### cantweight — 2013-03-04T18:01:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-sex-drive-in-women-i-cant-be-alone.6165/post-78921>

No matter where I search there is nothing on high sex drives in women only information on how to increase it. Am I alone in this???

I have a past history of mega health issues fibromyalgia, Lyme, Babesia, hypothyroid...possibly hashi positive ANA, elevated RH factor, parasites, apnea, candida, obesity, PCOS, 3 bouts of shingles, EBV, CMV.....seriously the list goes on forever I almost find it laughable....stupid conventional medicine.

That said I have supplemented, dieted, cleansed, CTd, rx'd...etc and regained my life. The things I can not get control of are thyroid, hormones, candida, and obesity.

I know I have low estradiol and low progesterone.....they were at menopausal levels. I am 38 and still have a period. Thinking I am a collector...I believe Dr Kruse called it....perhaps my estrogen is all locked up in my fat....just not showing in my blood? My testosterone was high...in the 90's, back in normal range for a year now.

Wondering if anyone has experienced or has insight into what would cause a raging sex drive? From day 1 of period to ovulation I am like a 25 yr old guy....its almost distracting!

Thanks for help and input.
Christi

### DarleenMB — 2013-03-04T20:33:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-sex-drive-in-women-i-cant-be-alone.6165/post-78932>

I had that same issue when I was in my late 30's-40's ... I"m pretty sure it was partially due to estrogen dominance and a high conversion rate to T. and distracting doesn't even begin to cover it. I swear I thought about sex then more than any 16 year old guy does. When my parts got yanked the relief was immediate ... I got my brain back. BAD way to do it though. Are you supplementing with DIM? If I remember correctly (and I'm sure someone will correct me if I'm not) it helps clear out estrogen metabolites. You may also want to consider getting labs done to see where your hormone panel is exactly. If you aren't producing enough Pg, that (I believe) can cause estrogen dominance as well. Labs will clarify and then you can work on treating. Trying to self diagnose without labs is just not going to work.

### cantweight — 2013-03-04T21:30:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-sex-drive-in-women-i-cant-be-alone.6165/post-78945>

Thank you! Yes! You put it perfectly....yanked parts= immediate relief and brain back. It is so crazy. Clears my head, helps me sleep, just getting out of hand these days! (no pun intended)

I am not on DIM but can add it to the mix, what the hell. Was on prescription progesterone, not pleasant. My hormones are not fans of me messing with them. About to start with Dr Kruse, see a hormone panel in my near future.

Just had to know if anyone else experienced this because it seems non existent in my searches.

### DarleenMB — 2013-03-04T21:34:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-sex-drive-in-women-i-cant-be-alone.6165/post-78947>

### Claudia — 2013-03-04T22:02:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-sex-drive-in-women-i-cant-be-alone.6165/post-78953>

> [DarleenMB said:](/goto/post?id=78947)

### cantweight — 2013-06-17T18:03:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-sex-drive-in-women-i-cant-be-alone.6165/post-88249>

I did have apnea obstructive and central. When I did my sleep test it came back moderate-severe by their standards. I had a CPAP, hated that thing so much. Did a round of hcg a few years ago and started sleeping again after years of not sleeping. There was a massive shift in my body and I stopped all the crazy snoring/breathing and slept like a baby. I go through periods when it is disrupted and really think there is a hormonal component to it. I believe it comes back when I have a hormonally active ovarian cyst. Just my theory on that but it has held true for me. Once I began to put the pieces together I can see a pattern over the years of sleep disruption followed by mood shifts and pain and irregular periods, enlarged abdomen; then either a surgery or a painful rupture. Damn ovaries!

### MamaGrok — 2013-03-04T20:44:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-emf-n-1.6166/post-78934>

I really made an effort over the last week to turn off my wifi at night to see if it makes a difference. I noticed a pretty strong correlation between the nights I forgot to turn it off, and the days that I had either "crawl in a corner and hide" moments or long, bad headaches. But that could be because I have a gut feeling about EMF being bad and was expecting an effect.

This week, dh is going to be in charge of turning off the wifi every night and back on every morning. He's going to turn it off most nights, but one or two nights of each of the next two weeks, he will leave it on. When I go to be sure it's on in the morning, I won't know whether he just turned it off 2hrs earlier (he gets up WAY before "needs 9 hours every night" me), or if he turned it off before bed. I'll keep track of my symptoms and he'll keep track of the wifi status. I'll let ya know in a week or so.

### MamaGrok — 2013-03-04T21:14:03+0200

And I'll test the cordless phones, although there are none on the second floor at all.

### prAna303 — 2013-03-04T21:16:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-emf-n-1.6166/post-78943>

The N:1 for me was the day i quit my work for about eight years ago... Everything in that business was wifi, GSM, laptops and late nights of work and drinks. Going from one day: gadget freak to another: hater of technology was the only way to go on living a life that i could be proud of.

### cinnamon — 2013-03-04T21:59:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-emf-n-1.6166/post-78952>

I plugged my dsl modem/router/wifi into a timer. It turns off automatically at midnight and back on at 7:30 am. I told my partner and his daughter they could turn it back on for an "emergency". In the 3 weeks so far, they haven't touched it. They both have cell phones and laptops that use wifi and one also has an ipad. They go to bed later than I do so the timer does the turning off while I am fast asleep. Having the wifi off at night is a compromise that at least should help everyone sleep a little better. (And yes, I think I sleep better with it off).

I have my computer wired directly in (ethernet) so I also turn off the "wifi" function on the modem during those times in the day when there isn't anyone else home to use the wifi. I wish it was all wired directly in but it would be difficult to add wired connections all over the house plus the cell phones and the ipad don't have ethernet cards so need wifi to work.

I wish there was something like that spray used by forensic evidence technicians to reveal blood that would enable emfs to be visible. Imagine if you could walk into a room and see that it was glowing a deep dark purple, getting darker near the corner... Maybe a kind of "x-ray vision" glasses? If there was such a thing, I reckon it would be easier to convince people to use things like wifi much more sparingly.

### MamaGrok — 2013-03-04T22:24:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-emf-n-1.6166/post-78956>

I asked dh (who is still active in all engineering fields as an intellectual property/ patent attorney) if he thought there would be any way to convert the existing coax that is available in every room in our house to transporting our internet signal. He said (confirming my thoughts, except I didn't realize something like this wouldn't already exist) it would probably require creating a specialty adaptor to transfer digital signal to analog coax (& vv) in a special way, but that maybe, if you had those adaptors at every coax outlet.

### Justfourme — 2013-03-04T20:58:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-labs-back-input-hack-needed-please.6167/post-78938>

Got some of my labs back. Vit D level was 31.1. Doc is having me take 50,000 IU of vit d3 every other day for 15 doses.
TSH was 1.5 piu/ ml;
free t3 was 2.06 pg/ml;
free t4 was 1.05 ng/
R t3 was 15.8 ng/do

Starting w supplements instead of armour.
500mg tyrosine
1-2mg iodine (kelife Norwegian seaweed)
Thyroid-mend start 1 capsule 3xd; then 2 capsule 3xd; then 3 capsule 3xd

Thoughts? Or do I just go on armour w/o trying supplements first?

Still waiting on cortisol and adrenal tests.

### Destiny — 2013-03-05T02:23:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-labs-back-input-hack-needed-please.6167/post-78990>

### Jack Kruse — 2013-03-05T03:13:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-labs-back-input-hack-needed-please.6167/post-78995>

### Hope — 2013-03-06T03:33:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-labs-back-input-hack-needed-please.6167/post-79248>

And when you're done with the EMF/artificial light, maybe check out [http://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-12-dare-to-disagree/](http://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-12-dare-to-disagree/)

and plug in your Ft3/RT3 into here: [http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/](http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/) you want that to be 20 or higher but looks like with your super low (is it really 2.06 or 2.6??) FT3 yours will be less than 20.

I would take the kelp no matter what...seaweeds are great...you can put them in water or broths/soup etc. You really need iodine....it looks like. If you don't have enough, you can't make enough of any thyroid hormones. I would try to up your iodine/kelp/seaweed intake...most people are deficient. I was extremely low and getting more sufficient has really helped (I also have hypothyroidism). The mitochondrial Rx would be good for you....though you need the dpi-paleo Rx as well.....

From BG 12:
The larger the brain the mammal has the more iodine levels in the dietary nutrients becomes critical. IE: The brains evolutionary construction is what controls the decision process of what foods we should be determining contains optimal nutrient density, not someone’s belief of such. Iodine is not conserved in human physiology to any great degree. This implies that our environment always had a deep supply, from an evolutionary standpoint. This foundational fact, is in many people’s blind spot today. It is underscored when you consider that human physiology has no source to concentrate or reserve iodine for future use at all in human physiology. A constant source is required for proper neurologic and thyroid functioning. I want you to examine it and realize why iodine is critical when you have a massive brain.

Here is a great blog on the different seaweeds:

[http://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-6-epi-paleo-rx/](http://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-6-epi-paleo-rx/)

So much to read/learn/change/do....

From a fellow new learner.....good luck!

### Justfourme — 2013-03-07T05:04:42+0200

# Thread: My N=1:  Earthing for sleep apnea
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-n-1-earthing-for-sleep-apnea.6168/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 21
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Optimalbound — 2013-03-04T21:33:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-n-1-earthing-for-sleep-apnea.6168/post-78946>

I was unaware that my sleep apnea might be caused by autoimmunity until Jack mentioned it a while ago in Ask Jack. Then, when Jack recently recommended earthing as especially helpful for those with autoimmune disease, I immediately knew I just had to do my own earthing N=1.

So I've been walking on the beach nearly every day for the past ten days. Bare feet and wading or walking where the sand is wet from the waves. The sea is barely above freezing here these days, so in the beginning I only lasted 5 minutes, but to my surprise I was quickly up to 25 minutes without much problems. Feels great.

Lo and behold, I have now had several nights of sleeping well without my CPAP machine! This is a big win for me. I guess it's too early to tell causation from correlation with absolute certainty, and I need further progress, but my N=1 so far certainly feels wildly promising!

Thanks, Jack, for leaving Ask Jack open and readable even for us lowly bronze members. Otherwise, I would probably have missed out on these little gems of information regarding autoimmunity.

### nonchalant — 2013-03-04T22:38:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-n-1-earthing-for-sleep-apnea.6168/post-78960>

Getting off the CPAP machine is a biggie. Glad you are seeing improvement so quickly!

### diane — 2013-03-04T22:49:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-n-1-earthing-for-sleep-apnea.6168/post-78964>

### Optimalbound — 2013-03-04T23:03:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-n-1-earthing-for-sleep-apnea.6168/post-78965>

Thanks, Nonchalant and Diane.

One thing that concerns me, is that I experienced a similar improvement last summer when I swam in the ocean regularly. At the time I thought it was the CT that did it, but the effect waned gradually when fall came and my CTing was in the cold tub instead of the ocean. I'm guessing that this reversal is an indication that there is something in my environment that I need to address... Which reminds me to schedule a consult with Dr.K soon. I've been procrastinating for way too long.

# Thread: Pentacyclic triterpenes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pentacyclic-triterpenes.6169/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### GlenPDQ — 2013-03-04T21:54:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pentacyclic-triterpenes.6169/post-78949>

I was searching for the effects of Pentacyclic Triterpenes on brown fat generation and found that ursolic acid, oleanolic acid, and betulinic acid have wide ranging health effects. Ursolic acid can increase brown fat, at least in mice. UA seems to convert between Oleanolic acid and UA in the body. Betulinic acid, UA, and OA are being studied for wide ranging health effects including obesity reduction and immunity. It is interesting that birch bark and chaga mushroom which grows on the birch bark in the far cold north have relatively high amounts of 1 or more of these PT's. Chaga is considered a medicinal or adaptogenic mushroom. Rosemary and apple peels are high in PT's, too.

Do PT's have a place in the overall Rx?

### caroline — 2013-04-02T03:09:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-where-you-live-matter-3-4-13-post.6173/post-82549>

I think maybe this is a sixth sense thing ...... where do you innately feel better? where do you long to be if you had the choice/chance? This isn't an overnite decision.....afterall it took JK three years to figure it out and he had a lot more information and tools at his disposal. Don't forget that Dr. K. is about 5-6 years ahead of us.

I really do think we know when something feels right - maybe that is over simplifying - and then you got to look at good, better and best situations for you and your family. We need to weigh up all the positives and negatives and figure out how to implement the changes we need to make. It also really depends on your health...as JK has said ...his day job is over the top awash in EMF's etc. and if he wants to live a long,healthy, productive life he had to do something pretty radical and I think some others here will as well.

I think BJ - here on the forum - is a perfect example. At first she was overwhelmed and scared and wanted to move immediately.... and now she has spent time and done some due diligence and checked her environment and weighed up all the negatives and positives and understands that maybe with a few changes - they will be okay where they are. So many things come into play for each of us ....and our happiness does have to figure into the decision too.

I think we all need to reflect on our happiness quotient .....the healthiest place for us might - in the long run - makes us miserable. so - Maybe second best??

There is so much to consider and it is not easy - but it will and should take time. We need to become centred and meditate and things will become clearer....

And we are getting more tools .....with the EMF expert.....

That guy who formed Microsoft and owned two sports franchises who lived in 5G land just died of a blood disease. Predictions coming to fruition. Seahawks’ and Trailblazers’ owner Paul Allen, co-founder if Microsoft, died from complications of non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma. He was 65.

### Jack Kruse — 2020-04-04T17:35:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-where-you-live-matter-3-4-13-post.6173/post-288345>

Living in NYC during C-19 was not a wise choice for millions.........when will they wake up and become Black Swans?
I call modern extinction events "Factor Y". We have had 5 previous extinctions. Modern man has learned nothing about any of them otherwise they'd realize some of the things we have co-opted to communicate are harming us during this pandemic.

What does the inside of the sixth extinction look like to a human? Here is that picture painted in words. A pandemic is like a wave or a surge of water. A surge is, by definition, time-limited. What happens when doctors can’t see the end of the tsunami? Despair sets in for them. Health is never more than one generation away from extinction. Few of us realize it. I teach my tribe to see the truth before it bites them in the ass. Humans forget how the rules of life are written by Nature. Extinction is the rule. Survival is the exception.

Though it might be nice to imagine there once was a time when man lived in harmony with nature, it’s not clear that he ever really did when you are a student of history.

We're so self-important. So arrogant of how our aberrant use of the electromagnetic spectrum fueled this virus. We're no different than the dinosaurs. Another failed mutation; another closed-end biological mistake. We just cannot fathom how our choices played the riptide in this pandemic that created the waves that are surging in our hospitals now.

Mankind's Dunning Kruger effect is thus: **IN PUSHING OTHER SPECIES TO EXTINCTION WITH ITS ABERRANT USE OF THE** _**electromagnetic spectrum,**_ **HUMANITY IS BUSY SAWING OFF THE LIMB ON WHICH IT PERCHES.**

That is what I see when I read this link below.

[https://www.newyorker.com/science/medical-dispatch/new-yorks-hospitals-take-on-the-coronavirus](https://www.newyorker.com/science/medical-dispatch/new-yorks-hospitals-take-on-the-coronavirus)

# Thread: NAC & Heartburn
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nac-heartburn.6174/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MamaGrok — 2013-03-05T04:40:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nac-heartburn.6174/post-79012>

Holy freakin moly. I just got hell in my esophagus. This happened a few days ago, I assumed I had maxed out on my HCl, took spoonful of baking soda in water, and it went away. It was BAD - I was listless b/c of the pain in my chest and throat.

As soon as it happened today, I knew why, b/c the only thing I've consumed in the last three hours is one cap of N-acetyl l-cysteine. Oh my gosh. Wow. Pain.

Just took the baking soda and now I'm burping. The burps just taste like I drank a bunch of carbonated water (probably a volcano in my gut, lol).

Anyone else experience this? What is NAC doing that is making this crazy heartburn? I just googled it and found people who have experienced this and they say to take it with lots of water. I don't know why baking soda seems to neutralize it if it's not an acid problem, but what the heck is happening?

I've been taking 1800mg a day for almost a year and never had this till this week. Today I definitely took it on an empty stomach (apparently a no-no), but the other day, it was \*not\* on an empty stomach. Both times were the final (third) dose for the day.

I welcome any input!

### TheKid — 2013-03-05T05:59:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nac-heartburn.6174/post-79025>

It is a mucolytic. So you have unwittingly compromised the mucous layer that protects the esophagus and maybe the stomach.

I just did the same thing about a week ago, and I am still not entirely well.

[http://www.thoracic.org/clinical/co...-there-for-copd/what-are-mucolytic-agents.php](http://www.thoracic.org/clinical/copd-guidelines/for-patients/what-kind-of-medications-are-there-for-copd/what-are-mucolytic-agents.php)

### TheKid — 2013-03-05T09:37:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nac-heartburn.6174/post-79046>

MamaGrok,

Because of the mucolytic effect, anything else that might irritate the esophagus and/or stomach may have an amplified impact. Coffee, for instance, is a well known irritant for those who's guts aren't optimal. So if you've been drinking Optimal/Bulletproof coffee, you may be throwing fuel on the fire. I am not sure what the best Rx is to allow the mucous to reestablish itself and for things to settle down.

My reflix has been so bad that it is now basically a GERD flare that is very resistant. I have actually succombed to taking a proton pump inhibitor under the advice of my doctor on a one off basis to tamp down on the erosive effects. I have a strong predisposition to esophageal cancer, so I don't want to play around. But I would imagine that from a dietary perspective, you could do a few days of "gentler" foods that might allow things to heal. If you come up with something, let me know.

### SeaHorse — 2013-03-05T14:23:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nac-heartburn.6174/post-79058>

What about aloe vera or slippery elm?

### ashryn — 2013-03-05T14:35:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nac-heartburn.6174/post-79064>

Marshmallow root too I think.

### MamaGrok — 2013-03-05T15:59:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nac-heartburn.6174/post-79077>

Thanks, TheKid! I haven't had any pain in between episodes, other than a tiny bit this morning. I don't ever drink coffee, but I do have a relatively benign cold that is producing a lot of mucus across the board. I wonder if that is helping any.

### MamaGrok — 2013-03-08T02:18:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nac-heartburn.6174/post-79568>

LOL, I have no idea what that means.

I'm so grateful for the responses on this thread, b/c I have not had one bit of heartburn ever since reading this. I make sure ever dose of NAC is accompanied by lots of food, lots of water, or both.

### Patty Cakes — 2013-03-07T00:26:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-hack-me.6177/post-79365>

Here are a few things I see (now that I have a moment to look at them):
1\. Thyroid dysfunction, although no current signs of Hashimotos (keep testing), but may be caused by something else since the TSH is OK
2\. AM Cortisol is low (brain disease) - sun in morning, Leptin Rx, no light at night, CT, EMF (bad for everything!)
3\. Glucose too high
4\. IGF appears to be good (in top of range)
5\. Iron saturation - whoa! That seems super high although ferritin is OK but serum iron seems a little high. Do you have hemachromotosis?
6\. Vit D is good!

You probably knew some of what I just said but I thought I would throw what I saw in the comments anyway! If you put it all together does it mean anything to you? Sounds like a consultation to me (before the big hibernation)!

I will double my milk thistle dose, make sure I eat b 12 foods, and figure a way to get some iodine into me, and CT the thyroid.. Test again in 6 months and see what happens... Does this sound sensible enough? Or is thyroid a thing that can't fix itself?

About the AM cortisol.. What should it be? I thought it was ok. It might be something to do with having the test 5 hours after waking that day, so it was almost PM cortisol as far as my body is concerned (wake at 5am watch the sun rise) test was supposed to be at 8 am but they kept us waiting 2 hours.

i don't understand the glucose score either.. I had fasted for 19 hours (unintentionally) prior to the test, and I eat no sugar, and less than 10g carbs per day. How could it be too high? What am I making sugar out of? This is all so confusing!

There is some haemochromotosis in my family, but I have never had a diagnosis. I give blood a couple of times per year which might have kept it in check, but I haven't for about 12 months. I thought to recheck again after a few months and see if there is an improving trend..

Do you think it is worth testing again in 3 months instead? I don't really know how long these things take to change either.

Thanks so much for helping

# Thread: To the Canadians - RT3 testing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-the-canadians-rt3-testing.6178/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### diane — 2013-03-05T15:08:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-the-canadians-rt3-testing.6178/post-79070>

To the Canadians on the forum, yes, I did get my RT3 results! Gamma Daynacare labs did the testing and I got the results yesterday. I know they're not in all provinces, but perhaps the lab you deal with will send to them?

Mine says 13.4 ng/dL (9.2 - 24.1). I have no clue what that means. LOL. But trying to look it up to get some info.

Just wanted to share because I know a lot of people are under the impression that you can't get RT3 tested here. Well, I found it!

### Destiny — 2013-03-06T02:38:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-the-canadians-rt3-testing.6178/post-79235>

> [diane said:](/goto/post?id=79070)
>
> To the Canadians on the forum, yes, I did get my RT3 results! Gamma Daynacare labs did the testing and I got the results yesterday. I know they're not in all provinces, but perhaps the lab you deal with will send to them?
>
> Mine says 13.4 ng/dL (9.2 - 24.1). I have no clue what that means. LOL. But trying to look it up to get some info.
>
> Just wanted to share because I know a lot of people are under the impression that you can't get RT3 tested here. Well, I found it!

Diane,

# Thread: NYT article - Negative reader comments can distort what readers think was reported
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nyt-article-negative-reader-comments-can-distort-what-readers-think-was-reported.6183/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ATL_Paleo — 2013-03-05T17:00:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nyt-article-negative-reader-comments-can-distort-what-readers-think-was-reported.6183/post-79099>

[http://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/03/opinion/sunday/this-story-stinks.html?\_r=0](http://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/03/opinion/sunday/this-story-stinks.html?_r=0)

The Web, it should be said, is still a marvelous place for public debate. But when it comes to reading and understanding news stories online â€” like this one, for example â€” the medium can have a surprisingly potent effect on the message. Comments from some readers, our research shows, can significantly distort what other readers think was reported in the first place.

But here, itâ€™s not the content of the comments that matters. Itâ€™s the tone.

\----------------

Simply including **an ad hominem attack in a reader comment was enough to make study participants think the downside of the reported technology was greater than theyâ€™d previously thought.**

While itâ€™s hard to quantify the distortional effects of such online nastiness, itâ€™s bound to be quite substantial, particularly â€” and perhaps ironically â€” in the area of science news.

An estimated 60 percent of the Americans seeking information about specific scientific matters say the Internet is their primary source of information â€” ranking it higher than any other news source.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-03-05T17:02:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nyt-article-negative-reader-comments-can-distort-what-readers-think-was-reported.6183/post-79101>

This is why I laugh when I hear docs say no one gets their info from the internet......my perception is 95% do now.

### ATL_Paleo — 2013-03-05T17:12:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nyt-article-negative-reader-comments-can-distort-what-readers-think-was-reported.6183/post-79104>

and the other 5% get their info from TV ads for food and drugs

### nonchalant — 2013-03-05T17:37:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nyt-article-negative-reader-comments-can-distort-what-readers-think-was-reported.6183/post-79106>

> [ATL\_Paleo said:](/goto/post?id=79099)
>
> [http://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/03/opinion/sunday/this-story-stinks.html?\_r=0](http://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/03/opinion/sunday/this-story-stinks.html?_r=0)
>
> Simply including **an ad hominem attack in a reader comment was enough to make study participants think the downside of the reported technology was greater than they’d previously thought.**

That explains it. The out-of-proportion reply to some new information. I would always wonder why people would give such emotional, inflammatory responses. It definitely seems to work in politics, also.

### kathylu — 2013-03-05T18:33:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nyt-article-negative-reader-comments-can-distort-what-readers-think-was-reported.6183/post-79120>

That must be why I was completely ignoring the recent dust-up here. I don't pay attention to the news anymore...

### Jack Kruse — 2013-03-05T18:37:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nyt-article-negative-reader-comments-can-distort-what-readers-think-was-reported.6183/post-79122>

me neither......it is a waste of good neurons.

### diane — 2013-03-05T20:43:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nyt-article-negative-reader-comments-can-distort-what-readers-think-was-reported.6183/post-79160>

I have to ignore comments on articles. I've read through them a few times and it just dashed my hopes for humanity. It's way too depressing to read them.

### DarleenMB — 2013-03-05T21:27:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nyt-article-negative-reader-comments-can-distort-what-readers-think-was-reported.6183/post-79176>

I gave up on the news 25 years ago. My attitude has been that if something earth shaking occurs SOMEbody will call me and tell me about it.

I also discovered the nasty effect all on my own quite a while back (which I dubbed the "torches and pitchforks" effect). Which is why, when I see the beginnings of trollish behavior, I run for the hills and don't look back.

### caroline — 2013-03-05T23:40:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nyt-article-negative-reader-comments-can-distort-what-readers-think-was-reported.6183/post-79193>

lesson learned......

> Well, one (small) city does - but it has to start somewhere. Hopefully the government in Toronto will wake up! But highly doubtful....

Very doubtful, since too much of the water supply comes from Toronto....unless of course someone educates the public and it gets put to a referendum....That is why Windsor and similar communities were able to do it, they don't depend on T.O. T.O. hasn't pronounced itself, it is sitting on the back burner still. But if a City as big as Toronto gets the fluoride out, it will be a domino effect, which is what I think the powers that be are afraid of. It's all PR....what would people think if all of a sudden fluoride was banned as a health issue when they have allowed it for the past 60 years , denying its deleterious effects? It incriminates the gov't, the dentists, the ministry of environment, etc...they all lose credibility? So now, people begin to question - everything? Can't have that...! because god forbid, they may question something else as well. If you read the comments section to the above link , you will see that some think that fluoride is needed for bacteria control??????? OMG, seriously, today there is no excuse for people being misinformed on any subject, **if they want to know.**

> Frost-resistant liquid format, ready to use right out of the bottle. Easy to handle â€“ brush or roll similar to ordinary wall paint - and still achieve an amazing reduction of 99% of HF-Radiation with only one layer. Dries quickly. Water clean-up.

### shilohman — 2013-03-06T01:24:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/who-will-be-1st-to-paint-your-bedroom-with-high-frequency-shielding-paint.6191/post-79205>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=79141)
>
> This is actually on our list of things to do - one thing you need to note - is once painted you need to ground the wall... DH is trying to determine if grounding the wall btwn 2 rooms -where the total sq footage is less than 1000 sqft is sufficient for both - or would we need to ground both rooms...

If the walls of both rooms are electrically connected, then you just need to ground at one point. If they are not, then you need to ground each room. Having an electrician install an isolated ground at the circuit breaker box might be your best way to ground the rooms.

So jealous!!!! your DH gets it about EMF...mine shrugs shoulders and thinks I am crazy.....he carries his cell as much on his hips as possible. UGH. I am trying to remember maybe he's just too broken....in his brain.

### Birdy — 2013-03-06T02:09:26+0200

### Optimalbound — 2013-03-06T11:19:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/who-will-be-1st-to-paint-your-bedroom-with-high-frequency-shielding-paint.6191/post-79276>

The Y-shield carbon paint looks like an excellent product but the documentation I've found so far does not say how well it works below 1GHz.

In order to make a proper faraday cage, I would think that one would have to paint the floor, too.

In any case, I'll wait for the EMF Rx before I start investing time and money on any given product or solution.

### HoneyChild — 2013-03-06T14:13:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/who-will-be-1st-to-paint-your-bedroom-with-high-frequency-shielding-paint.6191/post-79292>

Here's a cheaper alternative using "emergency blankets"...

[http://educate-yourself.org/cn/microwaveshielding09jan12.shtml](http://educate-yourself.org/cn/microwaveshielding09jan12.shtml)

I remember several years ago my dad installed "space blanket" insulation in the loft and then tried to get a TV signal using an indoor aerial but failed. That's when I realised that the aluminium coating was blocking the signal.

I also found this comment on a website from someone who's done the same thing...

> I recently relocated to a house that is 500 metres from a cellphone mast, and for the first time in my life I began to suffer horrible nightmares. Each night I would take half an hour to get to sleep, then have a nightmare and awaken within two hours and a half hours of going to bed. This two and a half hour cycle would repeat three times per night. Some mornings I would be nervous about driving because I was so shaken after the night before. After three weeks I realized the likely cause of the problem, and as an experiment I bought three highly reflective metallized mylar emergency (or "space" blankets) and fixed them around the two walls that face towards the mast with BluTac. This cost the princely sum of 11 euros. The blankets reach about 1.5 metres up the wall, I'm about half way that height above the floor in bed when I sleep. I took no further precautions - no earthing of the blankets, nothing covering the upper parts of the walls or the ceiling. It did the trick. The nightmares stopped that night and have not returned after a week, and although I do still awaken early, it happens only once a night, within half an hour of when I'd prefer to have awoken. When the camping shop has some more emergency blankets in stock I intend to cover the remaining parts of the walls and ceiling, but the worst is, I believe, over. There is only one other factor which I think might be connected to this, and I'd be very interested to see if it tallies with anyone else's experience. Back when we used mechanical, wind-up watches, I could never wear one. Even very expensive ones would keep terrible time and often stop altogether within a day of my putting them on. I've met several other people who had the same problem with watches over the years. I wonder if some of us might generate a more pronounced electrical field around our bodies than others, which interferes with the fine workings of mechanical watches and makes us more vulnerable to EM radiation by giving the transmitter something to act on?
>
> [http://www.electrosensitivity.org/cure.htm](http://www.electrosensitivity.org/cure.htm)

# Thread: CT use in CF patients?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-use-in-cf-patients.6193/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sjoshua — 2013-03-05T21:24:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-use-in-cf-patients.6193/post-79174>

My girlfriend is in her mid 20's and has Cystic Fibrosis: [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cystic\_fibrosis](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cystic_fibrosis). She is looking to try some new strategies to improve her health, and CT is one she is considering. I agree that it could be very beneficial, but I think the protocol may need modifications to assure it works properly considering her condition...

My concern is, I have not found any documentation of other cystic fibrosis patients using CT in their treatment regimen. Does anyone know of existing cases, and whether it was a positive or negative experience? Or, possibly other cases of individuals with other dibilitating lung disorders? Please share if so!

Since cystic fibrosis impacts the lungs so significantly (her lung function equates to roughly 50% of "normal" for her stats), my guess would be her ramp-up time (working from face dips -> ice packs -> bath) would be extended, and also her exposure time reduced. Obviously close temp monitoring would be used, but should she aim for the same minimum skin temps, or add an extra margin of error?

She is going to discuss it with her Dr's as well, but considering the medical community prefers the Rx bandaid, I fear that her docs will turn down the idea due to a lack of knowledge.

Any thoughts/comments pertaining to this experiment are appreciated, thanks for reading!

### Jack Kruse — 2013-03-05T22:23:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-use-in-cf-patients.6193/post-79183>

CT is huge for cystic fibrosis........especially in the ocean.

### Dali Dula — 2013-03-06T01:42:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aikido-and-ct.6195/post-79209>

I have practiced Aikido for over twenty years. Maybe this is why I found CT so accessible. check out this post I found from 2004 on a aikido forum.

[http://www.aikiweb.com/forums/showthread.php?t=6223](http://www.aikiweb.com/forums/showthread.php?t=6223)

William Reed wrote:
From "Ki : A Road Anyone can Travel" pg 211...Drawn from Shinto rituals of purification, Sen Shin no Gyo (Training to Cleanse the Mind) involves dousing the body with several buckets of cold water first thing on arising. This is done year round, even in the dead of winter...pg 247...Ueshiba (the founder) rose every morning at 4:00 A.M., doused his body with cold water, and then went outside to greet the morning sun, meditating before the Aiki Shrine for an hour and a half, feeling the communion of being in harmony with the universe...He would practice chinkon-kishin, a Shinto meditation technique for centering the mind and body, with gestures and invocations performed at sacred spots and shrines. He also practiced misogi, the ritual washing away of impurities with cold water, in streams and waterfalls.

# Thread: Addressing Technology Addictions
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/addressing-technology-addictions.6197/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 28
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fitness@home — 2013-03-06T03:11:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/addressing-technology-addictions.6197/post-79242>

Looking around today, I saw and heard so many kinds of devices. I heard pings from iPads in three different nearby offices and cell phones ringing. This is on top of the computer and/or laptop and phone already on each desk.

Speaking for myself, I know that my iPad has become an addiction of sorts. It is the original issue and was a gift. This tablet has wormed its way into my life deeper and deeper, until I was bringing it with me frequently. It has travelled to work every day and sat in its place of honor on my desk – next to my computer for three years.

This device made it easy to connect with my family/friends, send a quick message, check my personal email, look at posts on this forum, etc. I used to access the Internet through company wifi. When we upgraded phones I began to use my hot spot. \*_Bad, bad, bad…_

I took the day off on Monday. Spent some time reflecting on my addiction as I sat outside under the sunny sky, while lying down in the grass. The outcome is that I've made the decision to address the iPad addiction.

Step one: Admit I am addicted to the darn thing

Step two: iPad does not come to work with me any longer. I left it home today and survived (imagine that!) Nothing to distract me on my desk other than actual work… Well, my husband kept texting me on the phone. Finally had to tell him I had WORK to do. In retrospect, this might actually be harder on him

Step three: Cell phone will not be used for a hot spot. My surf time on the iPad will now be limited to a brief look in the early morning and some additional time in the evening.

It's a step in the right direction.

### Kiera2 — 2013-03-06T03:39:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/addressing-technology-addictions.6197/post-79252>

i had to google 'cell phone hot spot', my kids would be so ashamed LOL. Good for you; it sounds as though it wasn't as hard as you thought it would be.

I think you have much more of a life then me. I am going to want to work on changing that.

### Patty Cakes — 2013-03-06T21:33:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/addressing-technology-addictions.6197/post-79328>

This is hard for me as well. I don't have an iPad just a MacBook Pro that I use for everything. It stays charged for about 5 hours so I charge it at night and keep it going during the day. I carry it from room to room as I work on it. I know this is a problem but I haven't figured out how to get around this for work just yet. I'd much rather be on the computer than the TV. A few years ago I took to reading at night before bed so that has saved me a bit in my addiction to my laptop. My challenge will be to cut my time down....an hour a day would be tough right now so I am going to try for three hours a day. Hey, gotta start somewhere!

I'm sporting an old school iPhone 3Gs, which is also bad but I am not addicted to that. I forget to take it with me during the day and no longer get as many calls as I used to. I really only use it as an emergency item. Thank goodness something is under control!

### caroline — 2013-03-06T21:41:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/addressing-technology-addictions.6197/post-79333>

I love my laptop too. There is so much info to be had and I would miss that so much. We have to find a work around..I am not addicted to the laptop I guess - just all the information at our finger tips 24/7

Not to mention throughout the day it just seems like there are so many 'things' I need to check online: the school website, recipes, banking stuff, Whole Foods specials, the list goes on and on . . . I'm so used to finding out anything I want \*just like that\*

### diane — 2013-03-07T00:09:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/addressing-technology-addictions.6197/post-79361>

Yep, I'm kinda addicted too. I always have a laptop going - I use one all day, and read stuff most of the night. I rarely pay attention to the tv. It's a habit I'm willing to break, and I do have to consciously stop doing things as I can get addicted to those dumb Facebook games LOL. I've stopped carrying my phone with me everywhere as I usually have it in my pocket, and realized how close that was to my body.

### Patty Cakes — 2013-03-07T00:14:50+0200

Those are the words I was trying to say. Thank you for putting it so eloquently.

Now hours worth of work can take place in minutes. It's truly amazing and so painfully sad that the method (laptop) that has allowed us to access more in less time now causes the health problems.

### CarolMorris — 2013-03-07T01:21:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/addressing-technology-addictions.6197/post-79376>

Same feelings here...addicted to the quick access of information. Going back to "real" books was not too difficult...I love to sit in the sun, feet in the pool and read and Ipad and kindle not so good in the sun. I guess the days of floating on a rubber mat are gone....now that I will truly miss )

### Birdy — 2013-03-07T02:10:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/addressing-technology-addictions.6197/post-79383>

I guess I am more old school, but I carve out some time for each of the activities, and try not to do everything all at the same time. I found that when I was working and trying to cram a "hello" in every once in awhile, I had no time for either. I was at work for more and more hours, but working less and less, and felt justified in adding other activities because I was working so hard. But the truth is, when you cram your daily life in between the spaces of your work life, it is like you really never get to have a real life.

### Shijin13 — 2013-03-07T16:08:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/addressing-technology-addictions.6197/post-79465>

This is something that until we started keeping the wifi off - except for specific things (taxes, order b-day presents, supplements, and depositing checks) it was a big problem. especially for me. you really don't realize how much you get sucked into the vacuum that the internet is, until you limit it. Its gotten easier at home. b/c its now a PITA to turn on the router, wait for it to boot up, and then turn on the wifi (I still need to buy that multiple hardline router) so it doesn't get turned on... Its different at work though - I spend all day schlepping around the web doing research for my client - and short breaks to hop in here or FB.

I've really focused on keeping my iphone in airplane mode. I keep it in my backpack when going to-from work most of the time. I definitely have become more aware of being in the moment - when the wifi and phone is off. If only we'd all get back to remembering the important things in our lives - isn't the technology

### nonchalant — 2013-03-07T17:14:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/addressing-technology-addictions.6197/post-79473>

Guess it is a good thing I never got used to using GPS. My kids would be lost without it though.
I remember my father could add sums in his head very well. Like the total cost for a basket of grocery items. That always amazed me. And he seldom consulted maps, unless he was in a new area. Very good spatial awareness. But those capabilities were among the first things he lost, to Alzheimers.

### Huck — 2013-03-07T20:53:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/addressing-technology-addictions.6197/post-79502>

### shilohman — 2013-03-08T02:30:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/addressing-technology-addictions.6197/post-79571>

The most important time for absolutely no technology is while we sleep, best way to do this is popping the circuit breaker to the bedroom. For the times during the day when we can not get away from technology we can at least mitigate the EMF effects by grounding. The best way to ground is with a earth ground, but if that is not possible then grounding to a un-interruptable power supply (UPS) is the next best solution. Why? Because most UPS systems (the really cheap ones don't) take the AC from the wall and convert it to DC to charge the battery, then they convert the DC back to a square wave AC. The result of this is that any dirty AC is cleaned up and the ground out of the UPS is cleaned up as a result. What most people don't realize is if you are grounded when near EMF the skin effect of electricity causes the EMF to be shunted to the ground and doesn't enter your body. If you want to observe the skin effect of electricity watch this video [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sm6iCl\_qI1w](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sm6iCl_qI1w) and this video [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=t23iXhEiQUc](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=t23iXhEiQUc)

Note that the high power worker can touch the inside of his suit and the professor is able to touch the inside of the faraday cage with no ill effect. This is the skin effect of electricity. Now read this link (thanks to OptimalBound)
[http://berkeley.intel-research.net/arahimi/helmet/](http://berkeley.intel-research.net/arahimi/helmet/)

In the last link, if the researchers had grounded their helmets, then they would seen that the results would have been zero voltage on the inside of the hat.

So if you have to use technology, get grounded the best way you can.

### SeaJane — 2013-03-08T05:02:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/addressing-technology-addictions.6197/post-79600>

I just got a Trifield meter today and have been checking around the house with it so when the Hunter Fan question came up I checked ours out. Mine does not emit any electric field and the magnetic field is 1 (on the 0-3 scale) about 1 foot below the center of it. Interestingly, the emf at my desktop computer set-up is 1 or less (on the 0-3 scale). My kitchen is higher around 3-4 depending on what is on and my daughter's room is highest with nothing on (about 3-4) because the main electric feed to the house runs down the side of the house outside her room to the electric meter. Explains a lot of some of her struggles and why she is sleeping so much better the last year since she is now sleeping in our room. Surprisingly, our basement isn't lower. I'm thinking it is because the wiring is older--we had the main house rewired when we moved in.

Nonetheless, I have turned off the wireless except for 1/2 hour in the morning when my husband is using it, I unplug the TV, radio, computer when they are off and not being used. I have started turning the computer off for blocks of time during the day the last couple of days. That has been challenging and enlightening as I realize that it provided a false sense of connection with others. I've found that by having it off, I am connecting more directly with my daughter and my husband, I have called friends (on my corded phone) rather than send an email and have had brief and connected moments that are more rewarding than the sending and receiving of emails. I have a cell phone that I use for emergencies only so I don't have to break that habit. Overall, I am liking the shift in lifestyle and yet, still find it challenging to organize my life in blocks of time.

### nonchalant — 2013-03-08T16:05:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/addressing-technology-addictions.6197/post-79664>

> [kathylu said:](/goto/post?id=79588)
>
> It's bad! We have a Hunter fan in the bedroom too. It kicks out 5V and a magnetic field even when not in use. And another bad discovery...the emf field at the surface of our bed is pretty much zero (good), but at head level (standing up), there is an electric and a magnetic field...so something bad is coming out of the ceiling of our bedroom! Hot wires, I suppose.

Kathylu, does your fan have a remote control?

### shilohman — 2013-03-08T17:49:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/addressing-technology-addictions.6197/post-79672>

Unless you are grounded, EMF is entering all over our body's since we are being bathed in it. If your going to be wearing a TFH it absolutely has to be grounded otherwise it is just acting as an antenna for whatever frequencies it is tuned for. Same goes for any faraday cage, it absolutely must be grounded.

### shilohman — 2013-03-08T18:59:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/addressing-technology-addictions.6197/post-79677>

Grounding is very important and I know that many think it is difficult to do a work, but if your work place has orange colored outlets for computer equipment to plug into, then this is the perfect place to insert your grounding strap. The orange color means that the circuit is on an

> isolated ground

and things like space heaters are not allowed to plug into these receptacles.

### vkiernan — 2013-03-08T20:41:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/addressing-technology-addictions.6197/post-79688>

> [fitness@home said:](/goto/post?id=79579)
>
> I used to love using one of those noise machines with nature sounds at bedtime. I know someone who lets theirs run ALL night on the side table next to the bed.
>
> Has anyone tested a ceiling fan with a trifield meter yet? I would like to know how far down the field extends. We have a Hunter brand fan directly over our bed.

So the noise maker is bad? My dh has ringing in his ears so we HAVE to have a noise maker of some sort and it runs all night. It is by him and not me. How bad is this? Pretty soon I will have to move to my own room just to get healthy. He is not so sure about a magnetico sleep pad either. I have a lot of convincing to do. I did tell him I would try it in another room and then we can move it into our room when I prove it is good!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-from-average-to-awesome.6202/post-79273>

Hi all, I'm new to ALL of this, including forums! Wanting to become a life lover, and inspiration to my husband and my 12 month old twin girls.
My journey in points:
\-overworked and underpaid!
\-tired and anxious
\-history of binge eating
\-history of binge exercising
\- bad sleeper
\- history of counting calories and weighing food
\- wanting to be understood
\- sick of feeling overwhelmed and tired of not being who I am meant to be!
Would appreciate any tips or tricks on how to become a butterfly. I am doing the leptin reset and reading and reading and adapting as I go. May use this forum as an accountability tool if I can?
My stats are :
5'6
Not overweight but have 3 months pregnant banana shape.
32 years old.
Thanks everyone in advance, Jack I am discovering the wealth of knowledge you share- thank you!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ive-always-said-eat-fish-and-not-fish-oil.6205/post-79323>

I didn't even like much seafood before the Epi-paleo RX. I still am not an oyster lover, but I love how they make me feel. I couldn't have be happier when i gave up the oil for lots of seafood.

### caroline — 2013-03-06T21:16:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ive-always-said-eat-fish-and-not-fish-oil.6205/post-79325>

I have always loved fish but - man - am I having a problem with oysters now....and that is the one thing I should be eating by the bucket load ....

### freesia — 2013-03-06T23:34:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ive-always-said-eat-fish-and-not-fish-oil.6205/post-79355>

Tis Bluff oyster season here now Caroline. I've had my first raw ones ever this week.  C'mon you can do it!

I just used lemon juice, a teeny bit of seasalt and black pepper. I had some initial "hey-what-raw?-no-this-can't-be-right!" nonsense going on my head but once the first one was eaten (which was delicious!) I found they're kind of addictive. Though they're a bit pricy here!
I was surprised from Matt Lalondes talk at AHS13 that using his criteria clams rated so highly, when compared to scallops.
...Fresh clams are cheap as chips here.

### kathylu — 2013-03-07T04:06:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ive-always-said-eat-fish-and-not-fish-oil.6205/post-79420>

Keep in mind they are talking about the ethyl ester of omega 3 here. That's the active ingredient of Lovaza, a prescription fish oil. In order to patent it, they had to do a chemical modification not found in nature...thus the esterified version. Not the same at all as fish oil simply extracted from fishies...just sayin'. Sort of like a progestin instead of progesterone....

### freesia — 2013-03-07T08:04:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ive-always-said-eat-fish-and-not-fish-oil.6205/post-79433>

Thanks Kathy
Chemical modifications I can do without I think - unless they're cleverly organised endogenous ones...

### caroline — 2013-03-07T10:12:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ive-always-said-eat-fish-and-not-fish-oil.6205/post-79437>

> [freesia said:](/goto/post?id=79355)
>
> Tis Bluff oyster season here now Caroline. I've had my first raw ones ever this week.  C'mon you can do it!
>
> I just used lemon juice, a teeny bit of seasalt and black pepper. I had some initial "hey-what-raw?-no-this-can't-be-right!" nonsense going on my head but once the first one was eaten (which was delicious!) I found they're kind of addictive. Though they're a bit pricy here!
> I was surprised from Matt Lalondes talk at AHS13 that using his criteria clams rated so highly, when compared to scallops.
> ...Fresh clams are cheap as chips here.

The thing is Anne - I have been eating oysters for a year and didn't mind them at all but I got sick the last two times and now struggle really badly.
I had three this morning and three tonite - but not happy. I had a swig of wine right out of the bottle - couldn't wait to find a glass!! and then 2 dill pickles!!
what a gourmet combination do ya think???

Honestly I would much rather have a MHS .... i bet it would be a good idea to throw some oysters in that.... gonna think about that.....

So - clams are almost as good? I have been thinking of making a clam chowder. Are Shrimp almost as good too? I seem to be eating bucket loads lately.

### kathylu — 2013-03-07T17:01:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ive-always-said-eat-fish-and-not-fish-oil.6205/post-79471>

I much prefer clams to oysters. Someone needs to do a nutritional comparison...maybe clams are just as good? I hope!

### HelenaVon — 2013-03-07T17:34:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ive-always-said-eat-fish-and-not-fish-oil.6205/post-79477>

What about DHA from a seaweed source?

AND Are canned oysters okay to eat? I saw most are farmed from China( kind of a scary thought)
I think I might be able to stomach clams better but are you guys talking canned clams / fresh raw or cooked ones?

### janagram — 2013-03-08T18:22:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ive-always-said-eat-fish-and-not-fish-oil.6205/post-79674>

I have only been able to eat scallops so far....but long ago I loved pasta with clam sauce....hellllp! Let's find out which are best!

### Patty Cakes — 2013-03-08T21:29:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ive-always-said-eat-fish-and-not-fish-oil.6205/post-79696>

> [HelenaVon said:](/goto/post?id=79477)
>
> Are canned oysters okay to eat? I saw most are farmed from China( kind of a scary thought)

Canned oysters are a great sub for the fresh. Also a lot of them still come from Korea too. Not sure that's any better but as Dr. K says the worst fish is better than grass-fed organ meat. I love the Crown Prince Naturally Smoked Oysters and Napoleon Tiny Smoked Oysters.

### freesia — 2013-03-08T21:57:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ive-always-said-eat-fish-and-not-fish-oil.6205/post-79701>

Most local fresh shellfish here seems to have seasonal availability, so that's how and when I've been eating them. I love scallops but they were not rated as highly as clams according to Matt Lalonde's criteria at AHS12.
(You can find his talk on youtube.)

My mother said she got cravings for oysters when she was pregnant with me! They were just out of season so my dad brought home canned ones. Not sure where they were from, and this was a long while ago (!) but they had added msg and gave her a horrible migraine. I'd hope canned shellfish now wouldn't have nasty additives....just the good stuff and wash-offable oil hopefully?

\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*

For decades scientists have used only chemical-based approaches to regulate human cell growth and to determine what kind of cell a stem cell becomes in the body. Now fascinating new research is showing stem-cell programming may not be restricted to chemical signaling, that itâ€™s possible it also can be achieved by extremely low frequency signals in electromagnetic fields similar to the fields produced by the human heart and Earth.

It will be hosted by Institute of HeartMath Director of Research Dr. Rollin McCraty. The online event will be from 11 a.m. to 12 p.m. or 6 to 7 p.m. (PDT) Saturday, April 6. Dr. Ventura will discuss strategies for programming stem cells that are orchestrated by extremely subtle information delivered via quantum-field signaling. Some experts believe this area of research may lead to revolutionary strategies in regenerative medicine.

Development of a process akin to a "time machine" that is capable of reprogramming adult cells into other types of cells.

A finding that cells can produce acoustic/sound vibrations and the ability of cells to express vibrational signatures, which change depending on the task the cells are performing and which reflect the cellsâ€™ health.

The possibility that sounds emitted by organs contain information that regulates cellular functions at molecular, submolecular, and quantum levels.

Nobel Laureate Luc Montagnierâ€™s revolutionary experiment in which he purports that a virtually identical copy of a fragment of DNA in one test tube was "teleported" via electromagnetic signals to a second test tube containing pure water.

New research being conducted to determine if the results of Montagnierâ€™s DNA teleport experiments can be recreated at the whole cell level in stem-cell cultures.

Featured speaker

Dr. Carlo Ventura, M.D., Ph.D., is a professor of molecular biology in the school of medicine at the University of Bologna, Italy. Ventura is chief of the Laboratory of Molecular Biology and Stem Cell Engineering at the National Institute of Biostructures and Biosystems in Bologna. He has conducted a variety of cardiology-related research at several institutions and among his noted achievements is the discovery of opioid receptors in myocardial cells. Ventura has published more than 120 full papers in prestigious journals of biochemistry, cellular/molecular biology, and cardiology.

Dr. Rollin McCraty, Ph.D, is director of the Institute of HeartMath Research Center, Global Coherence Initiative (GCI) Steering Committee member and project coordinator for GCIâ€™s Global Coherence Monitoring System. McCraty has authored many scientific articles, papers and e-books and conducted a wide range of laboratory and field studies. His research background and interests include the physiology of emotions and mechanisms by which emotions influence cognitive processes, behavior and health. He is an authority on heart intelligence, heart-rate variability and heart coherence.

# Thread: OMG...love the pate' i just made!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omg-love-the-pate-i-just-made.6210/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### janagram — 2013-03-06T22:00:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omg-love-the-pate-i-just-made.6210/post-79339>

Lovin this!!!
Someone on this forum posted a recipe, I think, for chicken liver pate'! I remembered it was reall easy and I made it last night. It's so yum! Basically cooked livers(simmer about 3 minutes in water with onion slices and bay leaf) and butter in a 50/50 mix, whizzed until silky smooth. Oh, you drain the livers first...and add salt, pepper, and garlic powder. Now I can think of other yummy spices to add next time.....

### caroline — 2013-03-06T22:51:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omg-love-the-pate-i-just-made.6210/post-79345>

> Someone on this forum posted a recipe, I think, for chicken liver pate'! I remembered it was reall easy and I made it last night. It's so yum! Basically cooked livers(simmer about 3 minutes in water with onion slices and bay leaf) and butter in a 50/50 mix, whizzed until silky smooth. Oh, you drain the livers first...and add salt, pepper, and garlic powder. Now I can think of other yummy spices to add next time.....

That was me!!! It is so easy and so delicious! Did I actually post it? someone asked awhile ago and I didn't remember where it was ....anyway - I now make it with lamb's liver - It is stronger but still very good and lamb's liver is better for us. I just finished C/T and am shaking so bad I can't type lol!
I do still love it made with chicken livers and always take it with me if going to book club or friends so I have something to eat.

### Tjcam1 — 2013-03-07T02:47:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-from-average-to-awesome-down-under.6214/post-79393>

Hi everyone!
New to all of this and new to forums! Starting a journal to keep me accountable. Please feel free to provide (much needed) feedback, I love to learn!

Attempting the 90 day optimal reset!
5'6
58kgs
32 years old
2 lovely twin baby girls
1 fab husband!
Love to be inspired and hope to inspire!
No labs yet- anyone who can assist with directing me to the most basic level of labs would be great!

Goals- to love life, achieve self love and self acceptance.
To sleep better, be calmer, more energy and perform in the gym like a badass.

I start now!

### Jack Kruse — 2013-03-07T03:18:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-from-average-to-awesome-down-under.6214/post-79402>

### caroline — 2013-03-07T05:06:28+0200
